Preface   
The introduction of the competence-based curriculum (CBC) marks a transformative step 
in improving education quality and relevance. This Form 2 Computer Science Teaching 
Module has been meticulously designed to support teachers in effectively delivering 
Computer Science lessons under the new curriculum. The module aims to bridge the gap 
created by the absence of approved textbooks and to equip educators with practical 
tools, activities, and assessments tailored to the learners’ level and needs.   
As a Ministry, we understand the challenges that come with transitioning to a new 
curriculum framework. This module reflects our commitment to making this journey as 
smooth as possible for teachers, by providing structured guidance, suggested activities, 
and formative assessments that align with the CBC’s objectives. However, we also 
encourage educators to supplement this module with their own research and innovations 
to address any gaps that may arise during implementation.   
The suggested activities and assessments provided in this module are designed to foster 
practical and critical thinking skills in learners while ensuring relevance and adaptability to 
different school contexts. Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their 
unique classroom dynamics and to explore alternative, acceptable approaches when 
necessary. This flexibility is key to ensuring that all learners, regardless of their environment, 
benefit from meaningful and impactful Computer Science education.   
We hope this module will serve as a valuable resource for teachers as they navigate the 
new competence-based curriculum, enhancing their ability to inspire and equip learners 
with essential Computer Science knowledge and skills. Together, let us work towards 
creating a vibrant and responsive education system that prepares our learners for the 
demands of the digital age.   
    
 
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD.)  
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services  
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Acknowledgement   
   
  
The rapid evolution of technology has transformed every aspect of our lives, and 
education is no exception. In today’s digital age, it is essential that learners acquire 
computer science knowledge to succeed in the future. This Computer Science 
Module for Form 2 has been designed as a practical teaching resource for 
educators, providing clear guidance, lesson plans, and activities to help learners 
develop foundational skills, critical thinking, and practical expertise in an increasingly 
digital world. 
Aligned with the latest curriculum standards, the module presents real-life scenarios 
and hands-on exercises to support effective teaching and learning. It has been 
developed through a consultative and participatory process, ensuring that it is both 
relevant and user-friendly for teachers. Covering topics from basic computer skills to 
digital financial tools and creative problem-solving with technology, this module 
equips teachers to bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical 
application for their learners. 
We hope this module will empower teachers to guide learners in understanding 
computer science concepts while inspiring them to explore the subject’s limitless 
possibilities in everyday life. Our sincere gratitude goes to the educators and 
contributors who invested their time and expertise in developing this comprehensive, 
teacher-focused resource. 
We also thank all stakeholders and the Ministry of Education for their financial and 
technical support, and the staff at the Directorate of Curriculum Development for 
their dedication in ensuring the successful completion of this teaching module. 
Together, let us equip teachers with the tools they need to prepare the next 
generation for a digital future. 
 
  
 
  
Charles Ndakala (Dr.)   
 
Director - Curriculum Development  
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Guidelines for Teachers on Using Computer Science Module   
The Computer Science modules for Forms 1 to 4 are designed to equip learners with foundational 
knowledge of how computers work, programming skills, and problem-solving abilities. In the absence 
of approved textbooks, these modules will serve as comprehensive teaching resources for delivering 
impactful Computer Science lessons.   
   
1. Understand the Nature of Computer Science as a Subject   
Computer Science focuses on:   
• 
The science behind computers, including understanding hardware, software, and networks.   
• 
Developing computational thinking skills to analyse and solve problems.   
• 
Writing and debugging programs in programming languages like Python or Scratch.   
• 
Exploring how data is stored, processed, and managed.   
As a science, it goes beyond just using tools—it explains how those tools work and empowers 
learners to create solutions.   
   
2. Focus on Developing Computational Thinking   
The modules emphasise logical reasoning and problem-solving skills. Teachers should:   
• 
Start by introducing problem-solving techniques, such as breaking down a problem into 
smaller steps (decomposition).   
• 
Use examples like designing algorithms or flowcharts to help learners visualise solutions.   
• 
Encourage learners to explain their thought processes when solving programming problems.   
   
3. Teach Programming Through Practice   
Programming is a hands-on skill that requires regular practice. To teach programming:   
• 
Use the modules to introduce programming fundamentals, such as variables, loops, and 
decision-making structures.   
• 
Guide learners to write simple programs, such as a calculator, using the provided examples 
in the modules.   
• 
Introduce platforms like Python, Scratch, or Code.org for interactive coding sessions.   
• 
Encourage learners to debug their code and explore why errors occur. This reinforces 
problem-solving skills.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
4. 
Use Scenarios to Build Competences 
The module provides hands-on scenarios to help learners develop both understanding and practical 
skill. Under the CBC, scenarios should be purposeful and contextually rich — placing learners in 
realistic situations that challenge them to think critically and apply knowledge to solve genuine 
problems. Teachers should therefore: 
 
Present scenarios that reflect real-life contexts familiar to learners. Rather than abstract tasks, 
ground learning in meaningful local situations — for example, asking learners to design a 
flowchart for how a market trader in Lusaka calculates daily profit, or to write an algorithm 
that helps a clinic in Chipata manage its patient queue. This ensures learners can transfer 
knowledge to situations beyond the classroom, which is the core goal of competence-based 
learning. 
 
Use scenarios as assessment opportunities by evaluating the process and reasoning 
demonstrated, not only the final outcome. A learner who works through an imperfect solution 
but demonstrates clear logical thinking is showing genuine competence development. 
Provide constructive feedback that identifies what the learner did well and what specific 
aspect needs refinement. 
 
Encourage learners to develop their own scenarios for peers to work through. When a learner 
can construct a realistic problem and present it to others, it demonstrates mastery at the 
highest level of the CBC competence framework — moving from knowing to applying to 
creating. 
 
Connect scenarios to real-world applications so that learners clearly understand the purpose 
behind what they are learning. Situations such as building a simple app for a school tuck shop, 
writing a program to automate a repetitive community task, or designing a database for a 
local small business give learner a compelling reason to engage and help them see computing 
as a practical life skill. 
5. Relate Concepts to Real-World Applications   
Learners grasp concepts better when they understand their relevance. For example:   
• 
Explain how databases are used in everyday life, such as managing inventory in a store.   
• 
Discuss how programming is used to develop games, apps, or websites.   
• 
Highlight how artificial intelligence and robotics rely on programming skills.   
   
6. Foster Collaboration and Problem-Solving   
Collaborative learning builds confidence and teamwork. Teachers can:   
• 
Organise coding challenges where learners solve problems in pairs or groups.   
• 
Assign group projects, such as designing a game or creating a data management program.   
• 
Encourage learners to share their approaches to problem-solving and learn from one 
another.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
7. Focus on Assessment and Feedback   
Assessment in Computer Science should test both theoretical understanding and practical skills. 
Teachers should:   
• 
Use quizzes  to evaluate learners' knowledge of hardware, software, and algorithms.   
• 
Assign coding tasks and grade learners based on functionality, efficiency, and creativity of 
their programs.   
• 
Provide constructive feedback, pointing out errors in logic and suggesting improvements.   
   
8. Promote Innovation and Critical Thinking   
Computer Science encourages learners to think creatively and solve real-world problems. Teachers 
should:   
• 
Inspire learners to come up with innovative solutions, such as writing a program to calculate 
household budgets or create a chatbot.   
• 
Use examples in the modules to discuss advancements in technology, such as machine 
learning and blockchain.   
   
9. Integrate ICT Where Necessary   
While Computer Science focuses on programming and system understanding, ICT tools can 
enhance teaching. For example:   
• 
Use presentation software to explain complex concepts like data flow or computer 
architecture.   
• 
Incorporate online coding platforms to give learners more exposure to coding environments.

TERM ONE

TOPIC 2.1: PRODUCTIVITY TOOLS 
Overview 
This topic introduces learners to essential productivity tools, with a primary focus on spreadsheets. 
Learners will understand how software applications enhance efficiency in organising, calculating, 
analysing, and presenting data across various contexts such as schools, offices, and homes. By the end 
of this topic, learners will be able to perform fundamental data entry, analysis, and visualisation tasks. 
General Competence(s): 
This topic is intended to achieve the following competences among learners: 
Analytical Thinking 
Analyzing data, identifying trends and patterns, interpreting results 
from sorting, filtering, and statistical analysis. 
Application Software Skills 
Performing calculations, creating charts, managing data, and 
leveraging spreadsheet features. 
Collaboration and 
Communication 
Sharing spreadsheets via cloud tools and working on collaborative 
projects. 
Creativity and Innovation 
Visualizing data through charts and designing spreadsheets for 
practical problem-solving. 
Critical Thinking and 
Problem-Solving 
Applying formulas, functions, macros, and “what-if” scenarios to 
solve practical problems. 
Digital Literacy 
Navigating spreadsheet interfaces, using digital tools effectively, 
protecting workbooks, and applying formulas. 
Financial Literacy 
Developing financial models and analyzing “what-if” scenarios. 
Technical Proficiency 
Using advanced spreadsheet features such as macros, formulas, 
and data management tools. 
 
Hook 
"How do shops calculate totals and profits quickly?”  “How do teachers record and analyse learners’ 
marks efficiently?" Let Learners discuss real-life situations where organising and calculating data 
accurately is crucial. (You may Pose a follow-up question: "What happens if a single number in a long 
list of sales is wrong? How does a spreadsheet help find and fix that?") 
Key Terms  
Productivity Tools: Software applications that help people create, edit, and manage documents, data, 
and presentations to work more efficiently (e.g., word processors, spreadsheets). 
Spreadsheet: A digital tool made up of rows and columns used to organise, calculate, and analyse 
data. Think of it as a powerful, digital accountant’s ledger. 
Cell: The intersection of a row and a column where data is entered. Each cell has a unique address, 
like B4. 
Formula: A user-defined equation (starting with =) that performs calculations on values in a 
spreadsheet. Updates automatically if source data changes. 
Function: A pre-built tool in a spreadsheet that performs a specific calculation (e.g., SUM, AVERAGE). 
Functions are like shortcuts for complex calculations. 
Chart: A graphical representation of data used to visualise information and reveal patterns or trends 
at a glance. 
Macro: A set of automated instructions that performs a sequence of tasks to save time on repetitive 
actions.

Sub-Topic 2.1.1: Spreadsheets 
Introduction: This sub-topic introduces spreadsheets and their practical applications for organizing and 
analyzing data. Teachers should guide learners to understand the key components of the spreadsheet 
interface and explore how spreadsheets can be used effectively in everyday tasks. 
Learners should practise performing arithmetic operations using basic formulas and functions, and use 
filtering and sorting to identify trends, patterns, or specific values. Teach learners how to perform statistical 
analysis with functions such as COUNTIF, AVERAGE, and MEDIAN, and how to create charts to visualise 
data clearly. Emphasise data protection by showing how to protect cells, sheets, and workbooks. Guide 
learners to use collaborative cloud-based tools to share spreadsheets, create and apply macros, and 
develop financial models using what-if scenarios. Include practical exercises to help learners apply these 
skills effectively. 
Specific Competence(s) – Learners will: 
• 
2.1.1.1 Use spreadsheet for data entry and analysis. 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Discussing the Basic Components of the Spreadsheet Interface 
 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Choose any of the following but not limited to: 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom) 
• 
Classroom: Use board or charts to discuss spreadsheet components. 
• 
Charts and Posters: Display labelled spreadsheet parts. 
• 
Hands-On Demo: Learners explore spreadsheet software on computers. 
 
Technology Environment 
• 
Computer Laboratory: Learners interact directly with spreadsheet tools. 
• 
Projector/Digital Display: Demonstrate spreadsheet interface live. 
• 
Videos/Tutorials: Use clips to explain components and functions. 
 
Natural Environment 
• 
Real-Life Observation: Learners observe spreadsheet use in offices or schools 
 
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Choose any of the following but not limited to: 
• 
Computers with spreadsheet software (Microsoft Excel, LibreOffice Calc, or Google Sheets) 
• 
Printed screenshots of the spreadsheet interface for learners without computer access 
• 
Projector or large display for teacher demonstration 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods: 
• 
Collaborative Learning: Group interaction to identify spreadsheet components and understand 
their functions 
• 
Demonstration Method: Showing how spreadsheet components and functions operate. 
• 
Discussion Method: Sharing ideas on how spreadsheets improve accuracy and productivity. 
• 
Hands-on Activities: Use of spreadsheets to enter data and apply simple formulas. 
• 
Inquiry-Based Learning: Exploration of how spreadsheets calculate totals and reduce errors. 
• 
Observation Method: Viewing and identifying spreadsheet components during demonstrations. 
• 
Project-Based Learning: Creation of simple marks or sales records using spreadsheets. 
• 
Question and Answer Method: Guided questioning to reinforce understanding. 
• 
Real-World Exposure Method: Linking spreadsheet use to real-life situations such as school 
records and business sales.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Begin the lesson by projecting a live spreadsheet on the board (or distributing printed screenshots if 
computers are unavailable). Ask learners to silently study the interface for two minutes, then facilitate 
a whole-class "scavenger hunt": call out a component name (e.g., "formula bar") and have learners 
race to identify and click it. Introduce each component – workbook, worksheet, rows, columns, cells, 
cell address, formula bar, and ribbon – with a brief explanation. Consolidate with a labelled diagram 
exercise where learners annotate a printed screenshot. Close by asking: "If cell A1 contains 5 and B1 
contains 3, what would =A1+B1 in C1 show?" to preview the next activity. 
 
Suggested Learning Points 
 
Open the spreadsheet  
 
Explore the Basic Components of the Spreadsheet Interface 
Basic Components of the Spreadsheet Interface 
 
💡 Content Tip: A spreadsheet is modelled on an accountant’s ledger, but its real power lies in the 
dynamic link between cells. When data in one cell changes, every formula and chart referencing it 
updates instantly – turning a static table into a live data model. Stress this ‘live update’ concept early; 
it is the single most important differentiator from a paper ledger and will motivate learners to master 
formulas. Also point out that keyboard shortcuts (Ctrl+Home to jump to A1; Ctrl+End to find the last 
used cell) dramatically speed up navigation in large spreadsheets. 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 2: Exploring the Uses and Benefits of Spreadsheets in Daily Life 
Learning Environment Setup 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom) 
• 
Classroom discussions on uses and benefits of spreadsheets in daily life. 
• 
Charts and posters showing examples such as budgeting, marks recording, and sales tracking. 
• 
Role play activities simulating simple record-keeping situations.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
 
 
Technology Environment 
• 
Computer laboratory for exploring spreadsheet software and its features. 
• 
Projector or digital display for demonstrating real-life applications. 
• 
Interactive tutorials showing how spreadsheets support data organisation and calculation. 
 
Natural Environment 
• 
Observation of spreadsheet use in real settings such as shops, schools, and offices. 
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
• 
Computers with spreadsheet software 
• 
Projector and screen to display and compare platform interfaces 
• 
Charts and posters 
• 
Manila paper and markers for group brainstorming 
• 
Role cards for each group context 
• 
Sample worksheets (marks sheets, sales records) 
• 
Printed screenshots of spreadsheets 
• 
Activity sheets and exercise books 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods: 
• 
Cooperative Learning: Learners work in small groups to share ideas and complete the task 
together.  
• 
Role-Play / Context-Based Learning: Learners think and respond based on real-life situations 
like school, home, or business.  
• 
Brainstorming: Learners quickly generate ideas without criticism to encourage creative thinking.  
• 
Class Discussion: Learners present and compare ideas while the teacher guides 
understanding.  
• 
Demonstration: The teacher shows features of Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets, and LibreOffice 
Calc to make learning practical.  
• 
Experiential Learning: Learners practice using spreadsheets to gain hands-on experience. 
• 
Guided Discovery: The teacher asks questions that help learners discover spreadsheet 
advantages themselves.  
• 
Reflective Questioning: Learners think about and evaluate what they have learned at the end of 
the activity. 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Organise learners into groups of four. Each group receives a role card assigning them a context: school 
(recording exam marks), home (tracking a family budget), business (monitoring shop sales), or 
community (recording donations for a school fundraiser). Groups brainstorm for five minutes and list 
three specific tasks their context uses spreadsheets for, plus two advantages over manual calculation. 
Groups then present to the class, and the teacher compiles a master list on the board, grouping 
advantages into categories: accuracy, speed, and decision support. Conclude with a brief comparison 
of platforms: Microsoft Excel (industry standard), Google Sheets (real-time collaboration), and 
LibreOffice Calc (free and open-source). 
 
💡 Content Tip: Make the comparison of platforms concrete. Show learners that Google Sheets allows 
multiple people to edit the same file simultaneously – each user has a coloured cursor and changes 
appear in real time. This is a powerful selling point for group work. For Excel, highlight its advanced 
analytical tools (PivotTables, Power Query) relevant for future careers. For LibreOffice Calc, 
emphasise that it is completely free and works offline – important in contexts where internet access or 
software licences are limited.

Learning Activities 
Activity 3: Performing Arithmetic Operations Using Basic Formulas and Functions 
Learning Environment Setup 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom) 
•  
Arrange learners in a computer lab with desks facing the instructor’s screen. 
•  
Ensure each learner (or pair) has access to a computer. 
•  
Provide clear visibility of the projector/board. 
•  
Maintain a quiet, well-lit, and ventilated space. 
•  
Allow teacher movement for monitoring and support. 
 
Technology Environment 
• 
Computers/laptops with spreadsheet software (Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets). 
• 
Projector or large display for demonstrations. 
• 
Pre-prepared dataset (shop inventory table). 
• 
Reliable power supply or backup. 
• 
Optional: Internet access for sharing or Google Sheets. 
 
Natural Environment 
 
• 
Encourage questions and experimentation. 
• 
Promote peer collaboration. 
• 
Allow learners to learn from mistakes. 
• 
Use real-life context (shop inventory). 
 
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Choose any of the following but not limited to: 
• 
Computers with spreadsheet software 
• 
Pre-prepared shop inventory dataset (printed or shared as a file) 
• 
Step-by-step formula reference card for learners 
• 
Pre-populated spreadsheet (Item, Unit Price, Quantity, Total) 
• 
Projector/whiteboard 
• 
Instruction sheets (printed/digital) 
• 
Chalk/markers (if needed) 
• 
Practice worksheets 
• 
Sample formulas: =C2*B2, MAX, AVERAGE 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods: 
• 
Demonstration Method: Show typing =C2*B2, explain “=”, copying formulas, and absolute 
reference ($F$1). 
• 
Guided Practice: Learners enter and copy formulas, apply tasks with teacher support and 
feedback. 
• 
Inquiry-Based Learning: Explore formula changes and relative vs absolute references using 
guiding questions. 
• 
Hands-on Learning: Learners input formulas, use MAX and AVERAGE, and solve problems. 
• 
Independent Practice: Calculate totals, find maximum values, and averages. 
• 
 Collaborative Learning: Learners work in pairs to compare answers and solve errors.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Provide learners with a pre-populated dataset: a simple shop inventory table with columns for Item, 
Unit Price (K), Quantity, and Total. First, demonstrate how to type =C2*B2 in the Total column, 
explaining the = sign as the "trigger" that tells the spreadsheet a formula is coming. Then introduce 
relative references by copying the formula down the column – show how the row number adjusts 
automatically. Next, contrast with an absolute reference: add a Discount Rate in cell F1 and modify 
the formula to =C2*B2*(1-$F$1), demonstrating that the $ sign "locks" the reference when copying. 
Allow learners 15 minutes of guided practice, then set an independent challenge: calculate total stock 
value, find the most expensive item using MAX, and find the average price using AVERAGE. 
 
💡 Content Tip: The distinction between relative (A1) and absolute ($A$1) references is one of the 
most common sources of errors for beginners – and one of the most important concepts in spreadsheet 
literacy. A concrete demonstration is essential: copy a formula with a relative reference sideways and 
let learners observe how the column letter shifts. Then copy a formula with an absolute reference and 
let them see it stay fixed. A multiplication table is an ideal demonstration tool: the row headers use an 
absolute column reference ($A2) and the column headers use an absolute row reference (A$1), so a 
single formula in B2 can be copied to fill the entire table correctly. 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 4: Filtering and Sorting Data to Identify Trends, Patterns, or Specific Values 
Learning Environment Setup 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom) 
• 
Arrange learners in a computer lab with desks facing the instructor’s screen. 
• 
Ensure each learner (or pair) has access to a computer. 
• 
Provide clear visibility of the projector/board. 
• 
Maintain a quiet, well-lit, and ventilated environment. 
• 
Allow space for teacher movement and support. 
 
Technology Environment 
 
• 
Computers/laptops with spreadsheet software (Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets). 
• 
Projector or digital display for demonstrations. 
• 
Pre-prepared dataset (class marks with 30 learners). 
• 
Reliable power supply and optional internet access. 
 
Natural Environment 
 
• 
Encourage active participation and questioning. 
• 
Promote collaboration and peer support. 
• 
Allow learners to explore and learn from mistakes. 
• 
Use real-life context (analysis of learners’ performance). 
 
Teaching and Learning Materials: 
Choose any of the following but not limited to: 
• 
Computers with spreadsheet software 
• 
Pre-prepared class marks dataset 
• 
Printed challenge question cards 
• 
Class marks dataset (names, subject scores, total, grade) 
• 
Computers with spreadsheet software 
• 
Projector/whiteboard 
• 
Instruction sheets or activity tasks

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
Suggested Teaching Methods: 
• 
Demonstration Method: Show how to sort data and apply AutoFilter. 
• 
Guided Practice: Learners follow steps to sort and filter with support. 
• 
Inquiry-Based Learning: Start with questions like “Who scored highest in Mathematics?” 
• 
Hands-on Learning: Learners practice sorting and filtering to answer questions. 
• 
Problem-Solving Method: Tasks such as totals above 200 and identifying failures. 
• 
Collaborative Learning: Learners work in pairs/groups to compare results. 
• 
Discussion Method: Discuss why sorting/filtering is better than manual scanning. 
• 
Question and Answer Method: Use progressive questions to guide learning. 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Provide a class marks dataset (30 rows: learner name, subject scores for three subjects, total, and 
grade). Begin with an unstructured question: "Which learner scored highest in Mathematics?" Let 
learners attempt to answer by scanning the list – this surfaces the problem that manual scanning is 
slow and error-prone. Then demonstrate sorting the data by the Mathematics column (Data > Sort), 
asking learners to predict the result before executing. Next, introduce AutoFilter: apply filters to show 
only learners who scored "Distinction" in all three subjects. Pose progressive challenges: "Show only 
learners whose total is above 200." "How many learners failed Science?" Conclude by having learners 
remove all filters and explain why the underlying data is unchanged. 
💡 Content Tip: An important conceptual point to reinforce: filtering does NOT delete data – it 
temporarily hides rows that do not meet the criteria. The data is always there and can be restored by 
clearing the filter. This prevents panic when learners "lose" rows. Sorting, on the other hand, reorders 
data permanently (though Ctrl+Z can undo it). A best practice tip for real-world use: always keep an 
original "ID" or row-number column before sorting, so data can be returned to its original order if 
needed. Posing questions as problems ("Find all learners who scored above 80% in Biology") rather 
than instructions ("Apply a filter") builds analytical thinking alongside the technical skill. 
 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 5: Performing Statistical Analysis Using Functions (COUNTIF, AVERAGE, 
MEDIAN…) 
Learning Environment Setup 
Artificial (Physical Classroom): 
 
Computer lab or classroom arranged for pair/group work  
 
Clear view of board/projector  
Technology Environment: 
 
Computers with Microsoft Excel or Google Sheets  
 
Projector and prepared dataset  
Natural Environment: 
 
Quiet, supportive environment for concentration and discussion  
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Computers with spreadsheet software 
 
Class marks dataset (continuation from Activity 4) 
 
Function reference card listing syntax for SUM, AVERAGE, MIN, MAX, MEDIAN, COUNTIF 
 
Projector/board  
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Demonstration: Teacher shows how functions work  
 
Q&A: Engage learners before introducing formulas  
 
Guided Practice: Learners apply functions step-by-step  
 
Group Work: Learners discuss and compare results  
 
Discussion/Analysis: Learners give observations from data

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Use the same class marks dataset from Activity 4. Begin with a show of hands: "Who can tell me the 
average score without a calculator?" – then reveal how =AVERAGE(D2:D31) calculates it instantly. 
Introduce each statistical function in a "function gallery" format: display the function name, its syntax, 
and a one-line description on the board. Learners then apply each function to the dataset: =SUM for 
total marks in the class, =MIN and =MAX to identify the lowest and highest scorers, =MEDIAN to find 
the middle value, and =COUNTIF(E2:E31,"Distinction") to count grades meeting a criterion.  
Conclude with a comparative analysis task: "Using these functions, write three observations about the 
class performance and suggest one area the teacher should address." 
💡 Content Tip: A function is a pre-built formula that handles complex or repetitive operations – saving 
time and reducing errors. Familiarise learners with the "Insert Function" button (fx) or the "Function 
Library" in the ribbon, which categorises all available functions. This empowers independent 
exploration beyond what is taught in class. The COUNTIF function is particularly powerful and often 
underused: it counts only the cells that meet a specified condition, making it invaluable for tasks like 
counting how many learners achieved a certain grade. Distinguishing COUNTIF (counts cells meeting 
a condition) from SUM (totals values) from AVERAGE (finds the mean) is a critical conceptual 
checkpoint. 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 6: Creating Charts to Visualise Data 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical Classroom): 


▪ How to Conduct: 
Start with a discussion question: "If I give you a list of 12 monthly rainfall figures, how quickly can you 
tell me which month was wettest?" Then show the same data as a bar chart – the answer is immediate. 
Provide learners with a dataset of the tuck shop sales from Topic 2.1’s scenario (items and revenue). 
Guide them step-by-step to select the data, insert a column/bar chart, and add a title and axis labels. 
Then ask learners to independently create a pie chart from the same data. Run a "chart critique" 
session: project two charts side-by-side (bar vs. pie of the same data) and ask: "Which chart better 
shows which item raised the most money? Which better shows each item’s share of the total?" This 
develops critical evaluation alongside technical skill. 
 
💡 Content Tip: Charts transform rows of numbers into immediate visual insights, but choosing the 
wrong chart type can mislead the reader. A bar/column chart is best for comparing quantities across 
categories (e.g., sales per item). A pie chart is best for showing proportions of a whole (e.g., each 
item’s percentage of total sales) – but only when there are fewer than six or seven categories. A line 
chart is best for showing change over time (e.g., monthly sales trends). Teaching learners to justify 
their chart choice, not just create it, builds the analytical literacy required at higher levels and in the 
workplace.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Present a scenario: "You have spent two hours building a budget spreadsheet with 20 formulas. You 
share it with a colleague who accidentally types over one formula cell. The whole spreadsheet now 
shows wrong totals, and you don’t know which cell was changed." Ask learners: "How could this have 
been prevented?" Introduce the concept of cell protection as the solution. Demonstrate a two-step 
process: first, select only the input cells (cells where users should type) and uncheck "Locked" in 
Format Cells. Then, protect the sheet with a password (Review > Protect Sheet). Show learners that 
the formula cells are now locked, but the input cells remain editable. Allow learners to practise on the 
class marks spreadsheet, locking all formula cells and leaving only the score-entry cells open. 
 
💡 Content Tip: Protection in spreadsheets works in a counterintuitive way: by default, ALL cells in a 
spreadsheet are marked as ‘Locked’, but this has no effect until sheet protection is turned on. The 
correct workflow is: (1) select the cells you want to remain editable, (2) uncheck ‘Locked’ for those 
cells, (3) then enable sheet protection. Understanding this two-step logic is essential. A good 
classroom discussion prompt: "Why would you want to protect a spreadsheet before sharing it with a 
classmate for peer review?" The answer – to prevent accidental formula breakage – reinforces data 
integrity as a professional habit, not just a technical option.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Open with a class discussion: "Raise your hand if you have ever experienced this: you email a file to 
someone, they edit it, email it back, but by then you have also made changes to your copy – and now 
you have two different versions." Validate the frustration and explain that cloud-based collaboration 
solves this version-control problem. If internet is available, demonstrate Google Sheets: share the pre-
made budget spreadsheet with several learners simultaneously and show how their cursors and 
changes appear in real time. If internet is unavailable, simulate the process by assigning roles ("you 
are User A, you are User B") and discussing what each user would see. Discuss sharing permission 
levels (Viewer, Commenter, Editor) and when each is appropriate. 
💡 Content Tip: Cloud-based spreadsheets solve the version-control problem because everyone 
works on a single, shared live file – there is no "Final_v3_actuallyfinal.xlsx" problem. Key features to 
highlight for Google Sheets: the version history (File > Version History > See Version History) allows 
you to see every change ever made and who made it, and to restore a previous version. This is an 
important accountability and recovery tool. For Microsoft 365, OneDrive provides the same 
collaborative capability. Reinforce the protection tip from Activity 7: always protect formula cells before 
sharing, even with collaborators, so the model’s logic remains intact while allowing data input in the 
designated cells.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Select a repetitive formatting task that learners can relate to: preparing a monthly marks report (setting 
column widths, making the header row bold and purple, adding cell borders, and freezing the top row). 
Ask for a volunteer and time them doing this manually on a blank spreadsheet – it typically takes 3–5 
minutes. Then demonstrate recording a macro that does the same task: Tools/View > Macros > Record 
Macro, perform the formatting steps, stop recording. Name the macro "FormatReport" and assign it to 
a button. Click the button on a fresh sheet and show the formatting applied in under one second. Allow 
learners to record their own simple macro. Close with a safety discussion: macro-enabled files (.xlsm) 
can carry harmful code, so macros should only be enabled from trusted sources.

💡 Content Tip: Macros automate repetitive sequences of actions, saving significant time in real-world 
workflows. The Record Macro feature is the ideal entry point for beginners – it captures actions visually 
without requiring knowledge of VBA (Visual Basic for Applications), the underlying programming 
language. Do not attempt to teach VBA code at this stage; the goal is the concept of automation, not 
the syntax of a programming language. Practical uses learners will encounter: automatically formatting 
a monthly report, cleaning and standardising imported data, or generating a consistent output from a 
template. Always emphasise the security warning: files with macros must come from trusted sources, 
as malicious macros are a real cyber threat (connecting to Topic 2.5).

▪ How to Conduct: 
Frame this activity as a real-world simulation: "You are a financial advisor. A client wants to know: if I 
increase my monthly savings by K200, how much more will I have in five years? What if the bank 
interest rate changes?" Introduce the three-part model structure on the board: (1) Inputs/Assumptions 
– a clearly labelled section for variables (monthly income, rent, interest rate, salary increase); (2) 
Calculations – the main working area where formulas reference the Inputs section; (3) Outputs/Results 
– a summary showing key outcomes (total savings, disposable income). Have learners build this model 
collaboratively. Then demonstrate Scenario Manager (Data > What-If Analysis > Scenario Manager) 
to create and compare a Best Case (10% salary increase), Base Case (3%), and Worst Case (no 
increase) scenario. Run a Scenario Summary report and discuss the results.

Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: The separation of inputs from calculations from outputs is the hallmark of professional 
financial modelling. This structure makes the model easy to audit, update, and share: anyone can 
change the input values and immediately see the impact on the outputs, without needing to understand 
the formulas. There are two complementary What-If tools: Scenario Manager is best for comparing a 
small number of distinct, pre-defined situations (e.g., optimistic vs. pessimistic). Data Tables (Data > 
What-If Analysis > Data Table) are better for seeing a continuous range of outcomes driven by one or 
two variables (e.g., how do total savings change if the interest rate varies from 1% to 15%?). Always 
protect the calculation and output cells of a model before sharing, so that collaborators can only modify 
the input cells and cannot accidentally break the underlying logic.

TOPIC 2.2: DATA REPRESENTATION 
Overview 
Data representation refers to the way data such as numbers, text, images, and sound are stored and 
processed in a computer. Since computers only understand binary, different number systems and coding 
schemes are used. This topic introduces learners to binary, denary, octal, and hexadecimal number 
systems, and how text is represented using coding systems like ASCII. 
 
General Competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking 
Analyses patterns and relationships when converting between 
number systems. 
Application Software Skills 
Uses calculators or spreadsheet tools to verify conversions and 
operations. 
Collaboration and 
Communication 
Works in groups to discuss concepts and explain conversion 
processes. 
Creativity and Innovation 
Finds different methods or shortcuts to convert and represent 
data. 
Critical Thinking and Problem-
Solving 
Solves conversion problems and performs number system 
operations step-by-step. 
Data Structure Skills 
Understands how data (numbers and characters) is structured in 
computers. 
Digital Literacy 
Understands how computers represent data using number 
systems and ASCII. 
Technical Proficiency 
Demonstrates understanding of how digital systems process and 
store data. 
 
Hook 
The teacher writes "HELLO" on the board and asks: "Do you know that this word is stored in a computer 
as numbers?" The teacher then writes the binary for each letter: H←01001000, E←01000101, 
L←01001100, L←01001100, O←01001111. Learners discuss how letters, numbers, and images can 
all be represented using just 0s and 1s. (Ask: "If computers only understand 0 and 1, how can I play a 
colourful video game or see a photo?") 
Key Terms  
• 
Data Representation: The method used internally by a computer to store and process different 
forms of data using codes. 
• 
Number System: A writing system for expressing numbers, defined by its base (e.g., binary, 
denary). 
• 
Binary: A base-2 number system using only 0 and 1 – the fundamental language of computers, 
representing off (0) and on (1) states of electricity. 
• 
Denary/Decimal: The base-10 number system used in everyday life, using digits 0 through 9. 
• 
Octal: A base-8 number system using digits 0 through 7. Used in older computer systems and for 
Unix/Linux file permissions. 
• 
Hexadecimal: A base-16 number system using digits 0–9 and letters A–F. One hex digit 
represents exactly four binary bits. 
• 
ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange): A coding standard that assigns 
a unique number (0–127) to represent text characters in computers. 
• 
Bit: The smallest unit of data in a computer – a single binary value, either 0 or 1. 
• 
Byte: A unit of digital information consisting of 8 bits – enough to represent a single character.

Sub-Topic 2.2.1: Number Systems 
Introduction: This sub-topic introduces number systems as a way of writing and using numbers. The most 
common system is the decimal system, which uses digits from 0 to 9. In computing, other number systems 
such as binary, octal, and hexadecimal are used to represent and process data. Understanding number 
systems helps you see how numbers are used in everyday life and how computers store and handle 
information. 
Specific Competence(s) – Learners will: 
• 
2.2.1.1 Apply Number System Operations

Activity 1: Discussing Concepts of Hexadecimal and Octal Number Systems

Suggested Teaching Methods 


 
Group Work/Collaboration: Groups complete charts and create posters.  
 
Presentation: Groups explain their charts to the class.  


▪ How to Conduct: 
Begin with a brief analogy to activate prior knowledge: "We use a base-10 system because 
humans have ten fingers. A clock uses base-60 for seconds. Computers use base-2 because their 
switches have only two states: off and on." From this, build upward. Organise learners into groups 
and distribute a "Base Chart" template with four columns (Binary, Denary, Octal, Hexadecimal). 
Groups list the valid digits for each system and calculate its base. Each group then creates a large 
manila-paper poster of their chart and presents it, explaining why the system exists. Consolidate 
by filling a class comparison table on the board and asking: "Why would a programmer prefer 
hexadecimal over binary when writing a memory address?" – leading to the answer that one hex 
digit replaces four binary digits, making it far more compact.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: Connect each number system to a tangible real-world use to make them memorable. 
Binary (base-2): every electronic switch, from a transistor to a hard drive, uses two states. Octal (base-
8): used in Unix/Linux file permission codes – e.g., chmod 755 sets read, write, execute permissions 
using octal digits. Hexadecimal (base-16): used everywhere humans need to read binary data 
compactly – memory addresses (0x00FF), colour codes in web design (#FF0000 is pure red), and 
MAC addresses on network devices. Showing a real Linux terminal running chmod and a CSS colour 
picker using hex codes makes these abstract systems immediately relevant.







▪ How to Conduct: 
Structure this as a progressive skill-building sequence across three stages. Stage 1 – Binary ⇔ 
Denary: Introduce the place-value method for binary-to-denary (e.g., 1011₂ = 8+0+2+1 = 11₁₀) and the 
repeated-division-by-2 method for denary-to-binary. Demonstrate with a visual "place value table" 
written on the board (128 | 64 | 32 | 16 | 8 | 4 | 2 | 1). Learners practice in pairs and check each other’s 
answers. Stage 2 – Denary ⇔ Octal: Demonstrate the repeated-division-by-8 method and show how 
to read remainders from bottom to top. Connect to a Linux file permission context. Learners practice a 
set of five conversions independently. Stage 3 – Denary ⇔ Hexadecimal: Demonstrate repeated-
division-by-16, emphasising the substitution of letters for values 10–15 (A–F). Show how one hex digit 
represents exactly four binary bits by converting a byte (e.g., 11111111₂ = FF₁₆). Conclude with a 
"conversion relay race": each group member solves one conversion step and passes the sheet to the 
next.

Form 2 – Secondary Education 
 
💡 Content Tip: The repeated-division method works universally for converting from denary to any 
other base: divide the number by the target base, record the remainder, and repeat with the quotient 
until it reaches zero. Reading the remainders from bottom to top gives the result in the target base. 
For hexadecimal, the critical step learners often miss is the substitution: when a remainder is 10, write 
A; 11 = B; 12 = C; 13 = D; 14 = E; 15 = F. A ‘conversion cheat sheet’ (0–15 in denary, binary, octal, 
and hex) is invaluable during the learning phase. Encourage learners to always verify a conversion by 
converting the result back to the original number – this builds checking habits and reinforces the 
inverse relationship between the two methods.





▪ How to Conduct: 
Begin by connecting to the topic hook: "We know computers store numbers as binary. But how 
does the computer store your name?" Distribute printed ASCII tables and demonstrate: the capital 
letter A has the ASCII value 65 (denary), which is 01000001 in binary. Guide learners to convert 
the word "ZAM" to ASCII decimal values and then to 8-bit binary, showing their working for each 
letter. Add a personal engagement task: each learner converts the first three letters of their own 
name to binary and shares with a partner to decode. Extend with a mystery message activity: 
display a sequence of ASCII decimal values on the board and challenge learners to decode it as 
a Zambian phrase or motto. Close by asking: "ASCII covers 128 characters. What are its 
limitations, and why might we need a larger system like Unicode?"

💡 Content Tip: ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) assigns a unique 
number (0–127) to 128 characters: uppercase letters (A=65 to Z=90), lowercase letters (a=97 to 
z=122), digits (0–9 as 48–57), and common symbols. A key insight to emphasise: uppercase and 
lowercase versions of the same letter have different ASCII values (A=65, a=97 – a difference of 32). 
This is why string comparisons in programming are case-sensitive by default. ASCII is the foundation 
of text encoding in computing, but its 128-character limit is a real constraint. Briefly introduce Unicode 
(UTF-8) as the modern extension that covers over 143,000 characters including non-Latin scripts like 
Nyanja, Arabic, Chinese, and emoji – making it the standard for a multilingual world.

▪ How to Conduct: 
This consolidation activity uses a "conversion tournament" format to review all four systems 
simultaneously. Prepare a set of problem cards with mixed conversion tasks at three difficulty levels: 
Bronze (binary ⇔ denary), Silver (octal ⇔ denary and hex ⇔ denary), and Gold (multi-step conversions, 
e.g., binary → hex via denary; or ASCII letter → binary). Organise learners into groups of four. Each 
group starts at the Bronze level; completing all Bronze problems correctly earns them a Silver card. 
Solutions must be written with full working shown and explained to the teacher or a peer verifier before 
progressing. Groups post their final solution posters on the board. Conclude with a class walk-through 
of the most challenging Gold problems, asking different groups to explain each step.

💡 Content Tip: Multi-step conversions (e.g., converting a word to ASCII, then each ASCII value to 
hexadecimal, and then grouping the hex digits to find a compact code) are excellent synthesis tasks 
because they require learners to chain multiple skills together. The most common errors are: (1) 
forgetting to pad binary numbers to 8 bits (a byte) when representing a character; (2) confusing the 
‘from’ and ‘to’ system in a conversion (always write the base subscript, e.g., 1101₂ or 1A₁ ₆ to avoid 
ambiguity); and (3) missing the letter substitution in hexadecimal (writing 10 instead of A). Building the 
habit of writing subscripts and always verifying by converting back are the two most effective error-
prevention strategies. 
 
Expected Standard

of a long binary number.

Summary – Key Points Recap: 
• 
Computers use binary (base-2) because they have two states: on (1) and off (0). 
• 
Different number systems serve different purposes: denary (everyday use), binary (computer 
processing), octal and hexadecimal (human-friendly representations of binary). 
• 
One hexadecimal digit represents exactly 4 binary bits, making it compact and readable. 
• 
Conversion between number systems requires understanding place values and using 
systematic methods like repeated division. 
• 
ASCII is the standard for representing text characters as numbers (and ultimately binary). 
• 
All data in a computer – text, numbers, images, sounds – is ultimately represented in binary.

TOPIC 2.3: INTRODUCTION TO PROGRAMMING 
Overview 
This topic introduces learners to the fundamental concepts of programming through visual, block-based 
environments. It focuses on how to think logically, sequence instructions, and create simple programs 
using drag-and-drop coding blocks. 
 
General Competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking 
Analyses problems and breaks them into smaller steps for 
programming solutions.

Uses block-based programming software to design and execute 
programs. 
Collaboration and 
Communication

projects.

events. 
Critical Thinking and Problem-
Solving

variables effectively. 
Data Structure Skills 
Organises and manages program data through variables and 
sequences. 
Digital Literacy 
Understands programming concepts and how digital tools 
implement them. 
Programming Skills 
Demonstrates ability to construct programs using blocks, events, 
and logic. 
Technical Proficiency 
Shows competence in using programming tools and executing 
programs. 
 
Hook 
"Have you ever wondered how video games or animations are created? What if you could tell a 
computer exactly what to do using colourful blocks instead of typing complex code? Let's explore how 
you can become a creator, not just a user, of technology!" (Show a short, engaging animation or game 
created in Scratch to demonstrate what's possible.) 
 
Key Terms / Words / Vocabulary 
• 
Programming: The process of writing a set of instructions (code) that tells a computer how to 
perform a task. 
• 
Block-Based Programming: A visual programming method where instructions are represented 
as interlocking blocks, reducing syntax errors and making it ideal for beginners. 
• 
Algorithm: A step-by-step, unambiguous set of instructions to solve a problem. 
• 
Sequence: The order in which instructions are executed. Changing the sequence changes the 
outcome. 
• 
Loop: A programming construct that repeats a block of code multiple times. 
• 
Condition: A programming construct that makes a decision (if-then-else) – allows different code 
to execute based on whether a statement is true or false. 
• 
Variable: A named storage location that holds a value which can change, like a score in a game. 
• 
Event: An action that triggers a specific part of a program to run (e.g., a mouse click, pressing a 
key). 
• 
Sprite: A graphic character or object that can be controlled and moved by code. 
• 
Script: A set of programming instructions assembled to control a sprite or perform a task.

Sub-Topic 2.3.1: Block-Based Programming 
Introduction: This sub-topic introduces block-based programming as a way of creating programs using 
visual blocks instead of typing code. Each block represents a command or instruction, and you can drag 
and join the blocks together to build a program. This type of programming is easy to learn because it 
reduces errors and helps you understand how programs work step by step. It is commonly used by 
beginners to create games, animations, and simple applications. 
Specific Competence(s) – Learners will: 
• 
2.3.1.1 Construct programs using block-based software.

Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Exploration/Discovery Learning: Allow free interaction with the software to spark curiosity  
 
Discussion/Q&A: Learners share discoveries and surprises from exploration  
 
Demonstration: Teacher introduces main areas of the interface (Blocks Palette, Scripting Area, 
Stage)  
 
Comparison: Highlight differences between platforms and relate to physical LEGO® analogy  
 
Guided Introduction: Explain platform purposes and suitability for different skill levels 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Begin with a free exploration session: allow learners 10 minutes to open Scratch (online at 
scratch.mit.edu or the offline editor) and click anything they like without instruction. This removes the 
anxiety of "doing it wrong" and sparks curiosity. After 10 minutes, ask learners: "What did you discover? 
What did a block do that surprised you?" Use their discoveries to introduce the three main areas of the 
Scratch interface: the Blocks Palette (categories of blocks), the Scripting Area (where blocks are 
assembled), and the Stage (where the program runs). Introduce the concept that different platforms 
exist for different levels: Scratch and Blockly for beginners, MIT App Inventor for mobile apps, and 
Code.org for structured tutorials. Compare to physical LEGO®: blocks only connect in logical ways, 
preventing the equivalent of “syntax errors” in text-based languages.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: Block-based programming environments are deliberately designed so that blocks only 
snap together in ways that make logical sense – an Event block ("When Green Flag Clicked") must 
come first, a Motion block ("Move 10 Steps") goes in the middle, and so on. This structural constraint 
prevents the most common beginner frustration in text-based programming: syntax errors. Make this 
comparison explicit: "In Python, if you miss a colon or indent wrongly, the code breaks. In Scratch, the 
blocks simply won’t connect if they don’t belong together." This helps learners understand why block-
based programming is a powerful learning bridge to text-based languages – it teaches logic and 
structure without the syntax burden.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Provide learners with a structured "interface map" worksheet showing a screenshot of the Scratch 
interface with numbered blank labels. Learners work in pairs to identify and name each area: Stage, 
Sprite List, Blocks Palette, Scripting Area, and the green flag/red stop controls. After labelling, 
introduce the concept of a script by demonstrating a simple three-block script live: "When Green Flag 
Clicked" → "Move 10 Steps" → "Play Sound [Meow]". Ask learners to predict what will happen before 
running it. Learners then recreate this exact script independently. Debrief: "What did each block do? 
What would happen if we removed the event block at the top? Would the script run automatically?" – 
establishing that an event block is required to start a script.

💡 Content Tip: Emphasise the distinction between the four main block categories as a framework for 
thinking about code structure. Event blocks (yellow, hat-shaped) answer the question: "When does 
this code run?" Motion and Sound blocks (blue and pink) answer: "What does the sprite do?" Control 
blocks (orange) answer: "How many times? Under what conditions?" Sensing blocks (light blue) 
answer: "What does the sprite know about its environment?" This categorical thinking maps directly 
onto real programming: event handlers, actions, control flow, and input/output. Framing it this way from 
the beginning builds the mental model that transfers to text-based languages.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Create a physical matching game using printed cards. Set A contains concept cards: "Loop", 
"Condition", "Variable", "Event". Set B contains screenshot cards showing a corresponding Scratch 
block (e.g., "Repeat 10" block, "If <> Then" block, "Set [Score] to 0" block, "When Green Flag Clicked" 
block). Learners work in pairs to match each concept to its block, then explain in their own words what 
each block does and when they would use it. After the card game, open Scratch and navigate to an 
existing project (the Scratch community has thousands of examples). Learners identify at least one 
example of each construct in the real project’s code, annotating a printed screenshot with arrows and 
labels. Consolidate by having each pair share one construct they found and explain it to the class.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: These four constructs are the universal building blocks of all programming languages, 
not just Scratch. A loop (Repeat, Forever) in Scratch becomes a for or while loop in Python. A condition 
(If/Then/Else) becomes an if statement. A variable (Set Score to 0) becomes a variable declaration. 
An event (When Green Flag Clicked) becomes a main() function or an event listener in JavaScript. 
Making this cross-language connection early helps learners see Scratch as a stepping stone, not a 
destination, and motivates the deeper study of programming constructs. A useful classroom display: a 
"Scratch vs. Python" poster showing equivalent code structures side by side.

Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Demonstration: Teacher demonstrates coding a minimal program with think-aloud strategy  
 
Guided practice where learners follow along and teacher monitors progress  
 
Independent Practice: Independent extension of the project with additional movement, 
boundaries, and sprite actions  
 
Observation & Feedback: Teacher checks learners’ work and provides guidance  


▪ How to Conduct: 
Use the "I Do, We Do, You Do" instructional structure. I Do: Teacher live-codes a Minimal Viable 
Product (MVP) – a sprite that moves right when the right arrow key is pressed and left when the left 
arrow key is pressed. Think aloud throughout: "First I need an event block to detect the key press. 
Then I need a motion block. Let me test it after each block I add." We Do: Learners follow along step-
by-step from the board to recreate the same project, with the teacher checking progress at each step. 
You Do: Learners independently extend the project by adding: (a) up and down movement, (b) a 
boundary so the sprite cannot go off-screen (using an If on edge, bounce block or a conditional), and 
(c) a costume change when the sprite moves. The independence challenge – extending beyond the 
guided steps – is where genuine computational thinking develops.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
 
💡 Content Tip: "Start simple and add features" is the core discipline of software development. Build 
a Minimal Viable Product (MVP) first – the smallest version that does something useful – then add 
complexity incrementally. Test after every addition. This practice directly parallels professional 
software development methodology and prevents "spaghetti code" (an overwhelming, untestable 
tangle of blocks). A practical debugging habit to model from the first lesson: if the program is not 
working, do not delete everything and start over. Instead, check the sequence (are the blocks in the 
right order?), check the connections (are all blocks attached?), and test the simplest possible version 
first, then add back components one by one until the error reappears.

 Suggested Teaching Methods

▪ How to Conduct: 
Guide learners to build a simple interactive story or game that explicitly requires all four constructs. 
Suggested project: "Zambia Nature Quiz" – a sprite asks a multiple-choice question about a 
Zambian animal; the learner presses 1, 2, or 3; the sprite responds with correct/incorrect feedback; 
a Score variable increases by 1 for each correct answer; and a Forever loop keeps the quiz 
running. Before coding, learners complete a planning sheet listing: sprites needed, variables 
needed, events that trigger actions, and conditions that control branching. After building, introduce 
the concept of debugging by intentionally placing the Score variable increment block in the wrong 
place (outside the If block) and challenging learners to identify and fix it. Conclude with a mini 
peer-showcase.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
 
💡 Content Tip: Planning before coding is one of the most important habits to instil. A project plan does 
not need to be formal – even a simple list of "sprites I need, variables I need, and events that happen" 
prevents learners from starting with no direction and becoming overwhelmed. This planning mindset 
maps directly onto professional software development practices (user stories, pseudocode, 
wireframes). For debugging practice, intentional bugs are more pedagogically effective than accidental 
ones: the teacher controls the type and complexity of the bug, and it ensures all learners encounter 
the same problem simultaneously. Common bugs to introduce: a variable not being reset when the 
game starts (add "Set Score to 0" under the Green Flag event); a condition check with the wrong 
operator (greater than vs. less than); or a sound block placed outside a loop so it only plays once.

TOPIC 2.4: NETWORKING 
Overview 
Computer networking is the process of connecting multiple computing devices to enable the sharing of 
data, software, and hardware resources. This topic focuses on network topologies – the design and layout 
of network connections – and how they determine how information flows across a Local Area Network 
(LAN). 
 
General Competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking 
Analyses different network topologies and evaluates their 
suitability for specific scenarios. 
Collaboration and 
Communication 
Works in teams to design, build, and configure networks while 
clearly explaining decisions. 
Creativity and Innovation 
Designs original and efficient network layouts to solve connectivity 
challenges. 
Critical Thinking and Problem-
Solving 
Identifies and resolves network design or configuration problems 
logically. 
Technical Proficiency 
Demonstrates hands-on skills in building, configuring, and 
troubleshooting networks. 
Digital Literacy 
Understands networking concepts, devices, protocols, and their 
practical applications. 
 
Hook 
"How can a single printer in the school office be used by every teacher without anyone 
leaving their classroom?" (Follow up: "What happens if the cable to that printer breaks? 
Can anyone print then?") 
 
Key Terms  
Topology: The physical or logical layout of devices and connections in a network – the “map” of the 
network. 
Ethernet: The most common technology for connecting devices in a wired Local Area Network (LAN). 
UTP / STP (Unshielded/Shielded Twisted Pair): Copper cabling used in Ethernet networks. STP has 
extra shielding to reduce interference in noisy environments. 
IP Address: A unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network for communication and 
identification. 
LAN (Local Area Network): A network that connects devices within a limited area, such as a school, 
home, or office building. 
Router: A device that connects different networks together and directs traffic between them. 
Switch: A device that connects multiple devices on the same network and forwards data only to its 
intended destination. 
 
Sub-Topic 2.4.1: Network Topologies 
Introduction: This sub-topic introduces network topologies as the different ways computers and devices 
are arranged and connected in a network. These arrangements determine how data moves from one 
device to another. Common types of network topologies include bus, star, ring, mesh, and tree. 
Understanding network topologies helps you know how networks are organised and how they work 
efficiently 
Specific Competence(s) – Learners will: 
• 
2.4.1.1 Develop and configure network topologies.

Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Discussing Different Types of Network Topologies 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 
 
Classroom arranged for movement and group interaction to model physical networks  
 
Space to safely form Star, Bus, Ring, and Mesh topologies with string and learners  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Projector or screen to display diagrams of network topologies after the physical activity  
 
Computers for reference to real network layouts  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Supportive, interactive environment encouraging hands-on exploration, observation, and 
discussion  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Strings or ropes for the kinaesthetic human network activity to model network connections  
 
Labels or badges for roles (switch, computer, server)  
 
Projector/board for diagrams  
 
Printed network topology diagrams (Star, Bus, Ring, Mesh) 
 
Pens, markers, and paper for notes and Worksheets or activity sheets for reflection  
 
Network simulation software (e.g., Cisco Packet Tracer) for follow-up 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Kinesthetic Learning: Physically model each network topology using learners and strings  
 
Demonstration: Show diagrams and explain the structure after the hands-on activity  
 
Discussion/Questioning: Compare topologies and discuss advantages, limitations, and real-life 
applications  
 
Reflection: Ask learners to identify which topology matches their school’s lab network  
 
Collaborative Learning: Encourage teamwork while forming topologies and passing messages  
▪ How to Conduct: 
Use a kinaesthetic "human network" activity before showing any diagrams. Arrange learners physically 
to model each topology using lengths of string as cables and learners as computers. For a Star 
topology: one learner stands in the centre (the switch) and holds a piece of string to each of the other 
learners; all communication goes through the centre. For a Bus topology: learners stand in a line and 
pass a message (a piece of paper) along a single string – show that if the string breaks anywhere, 
nobody further down can communicate. For a Ring topology: learners form a circle, each holding both 
ends of adjacent strings. For a Mesh topology: every learner holds strings to multiple others. After each 
physical model, show the corresponding network diagram and identify the labels. Conclude by 
discussing: "Which topology best describes your school’s computer lab network?" 
💡 Content Tip: Each topology involves a fundamental trade-off between cost, reliability, and ease of 
management. Star topology: all devices connect to a central switch. If one cable fails, only that device 
is affected – making it easy to troubleshoot. However, if the switch fails, the entire network goes down. 
This is the most common topology in schools and offices. Bus topology: all devices share a single 
backbone cable. It is cheap and simple but highly vulnerable – a break anywhere on the backbone 
disconnects all devices. Ring topology: data travels in one direction around a loop. A break anywhere 
disrupts the entire network unless a dual-ring is used. Mesh topology: every device connects to every 
other device. It is extremely resilient (multiple alternative paths for data) but very expensive due to the 
number of cables required. Mesh is used in critical infrastructure like military networks and internet 
backbone connections.

Activity 2: Analysing Roles of Network Topology 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 


 
Space for group debate and peer feedback  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Projector/board to display scenario instructions or sample diagrams

Computers or tablets for research on hardware requirements and topology types  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Collaborative, reflective environment encouraging analysis, critical thinking, and structured 
debate  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Scenario cards describing different network environments  
 
Worksheets or report templates for recommendations  
 
Projector/board for visual prompts and diagram examples  
 
Pens, markers, and paper for calculations and notes  
 
Optional reference charts for hardware requirements and topology features  
 
Topology recommendation report template 
 
Cost-benefit analysis comparison table (printed) 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Collaborative Learning: Groups analyse scenarios and complete recommendation reports  
 
Problem-Based Learning: Apply knowledge of topologies to real-world situations  
 
Presentation & Debate: Groups present their solution and discuss its advantages and risks  
 
Guided Discussion: Teacher facilitates comparison of different topologies and decision-making 
criteria  
 
Critical Thinking: Evaluate pros, cons, and risks to justify topology choices 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Present learners with three scenario cards describing real-world environments: (A) a 30-computer 
school computer lab in a single room with a full-time technician; (B) a small rural clinic with four 
computers in separate rooms and a limited budget; (C) a large bank with 200 workstations across three 
floors that cannot afford any network downtime. Groups of three analyse their scenario card and 
complete a topology recommendation report: (1) Which topology do you recommend? (2) What 
hardware would you need (how many switches, how much cable)? (3) What are the main risks of this 
topology for this scenario? (4) Why is it better than the alternatives? Groups present their 
recommendation to the class, and the class votes on whether they agree with the choice, generating 
a structured debate. 
💡 Content Tip: Topology selection is fundamentally an engineering decision that balances four 
factors: cost (how much cable and how many network devices?), reliability (what happens when a 
single component fails?), scalability (how easily can new devices be added?), and performance (does 
the topology create bottlenecks?). Provide learners with a simple cost-benefit analysis table to fill in 
for each topology across these four dimensions. The key insight is that no topology is universally "best" 
– the correct answer always depends on the specific constraints and requirements of the environment. 
This is a powerful lesson in applied problem-solving that extends beyond networking to engineering 
and design thinking.

Activity 3: Creating Network Cables (Ethernet, UTP, STP) 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 
 
Classroom or lab arranged with individual workstations for hands-on cable creation  
 
Clear space for teacher demonstration and safe handling of tools  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Projector or screen to show video demonstrations  
 
Cable testers and computers for testing network cables  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Safe, structured, and supervised environment promoting practical skill development and attention 
to safety  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
RJ45 connectors, UTP/STP cables, scissors, cable strippers, crimping tools  
 
Printed T568B wiring diagrams and colour-order charts  
 
Cable testers for verification  
 
Projector/board for video demonstration and step-by-step instructions  
 
Activity sheets to record cable test results and errors  
 
Networking toolkits per group: UTP/STP cable, RJ45 connectors, crimping tools, cable 
strippers 
 
Safety guidelines handout 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Demonstration: Teacher shows cable-crimping steps and safety precautions  
 
Guided Practice: Learners follow step-by-step instructions to make a cable  
 
Collaborative Learning: Pair work to memorise T568B colour order and check each other’s 
work  
 
Hands-On Practice: Each learner creates, tests, and corrects their own cable  
 
Problem-Solving: Identify errors in failed cables and correct them 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Before allowing learners to handle tools, show a two-minute video demonstrating the crimping process 
and the T568B wiring standard. Discuss safety: RJ45 connectors have sharp edges; scissors and 
cable strippers must be handled carefully. Distribute a printed T568B colour-order diagram 
(White/Orange, Orange, White/Green, Blue, White/Blue, Green, White/Brown, Brown) and ask 
learners to memorise the order by colour in pairs. Then demonstrate live: (1) Strip 2.5 cm of the outer 
jacket without cutting the inner wires; (2) Untwist and straighten the wires; (3) Arrange in T568B order; 
(4) Trim to 1.5 cm; (5) Insert into the RJ45 connector, ensuring all eight wires reach the end; (6) Crimp 
firmly. Learners then make their own cable. Test every cable with a cable tester and record results 
(pass/fail for each of the 8 pins). Any failed cable is disassembled, the error identified, and the cable 
remade. 
💡 Content Tip: Three practical tips for successful cable crimping that save time and materials: First, 
after untwisting the wires, run your thumbnail along each wire to straighten it – this makes ordering 
them much easier. Second, after arranging the wires in the correct order, hold them tightly between 
your thumb and forefinger and make a single clean cut to ensure all eight ends are level before 
inserting into the RJ45 connector – uneven ends are the most common cause of cable failures. Third, 
before crimping, look through the transparent RJ45 connector housing and verify that all eight wires 
are visible at the front edge – if any wire is short, pull them out and adjust. A cable tester is non-
negotiable: always test before installing a cable in a network to avoid damaging connected equipment.

Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Collaborative Learning: Groups design networks together using printed stamps before finalising 
diagrams  
 
Guided Practice: Teacher provides hints on topology selection, switch count, and IP allocation  
 
Peer Review: Another group evaluates the diagram for clarity, correctness, and completeness  
 
Hands-On Learning: Physically arranging components before drawing reinforces spatial 
understanding  
 
Problem-Solving: Decide on topologies and component placement to meet scenario 
requirements 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Provide each group with a scenario brief and a set of printed network component "stamps" (icons of 
computers, switches, routers, printers, and servers) that they can arrange physically on paper before 
drawing their final diagram. The scenario: "Design a network for a new secondary school with 2 
computer labs (15 computers each), a library (5 computers), a staff room (8 computers), and a 
principal’s office (2 computers). The school has a single internet connection." Groups must: (1) decide 
on the topology for each section and for connecting sections together; (2) calculate the minimum 
number of switches needed; (3) draw a labelled network diagram using standard symbols; (4) annotate 
with IP address ranges for each section (e.g., Lab 1 uses 192.168.1.x). Diagrams are reviewed by 
another group who must answer: "Can you identify what every component is and why it is placed 
there?" 
💡 Content Tip: Professional network diagrams use standardised symbols (Cisco icon set is the 
industry standard) and always include: device labels, IP address schemes, cable types, and 
connection ports. For this level, the critical conceptual point is the hierarchical network structure used 
in schools and offices: multiple switches (access layer) connect workstations to a central, higher-
capacity switch or router (distribution/core layer) which connects to the internet. This hierarchical model 
is more scalable and easier to manage than connecting everything to a single switch. When assigning 
IP addresses, explain the concept of subnets: grouping all devices in Lab 1 under 192.168.1.x and Lab 
2 under 192.168.2.x makes the network easier to manage and troubleshoot.

Activity 5: Creating and Configuring Network Topologies (Simulation) 
 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 


Technology Environment: 

