 
Projector/board to demonstrate placement, configuration, and troubleshooting steps  
 
Internet optional for reference and troubleshooting guides  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Structured, supportive environment promoting hands-on experimentation, troubleshooting, and 
logical thinking  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Computers with Cisco Packet Tracer installed (free from Cisco NetAcad) 
 
Projector/board for live demonstration, Printed diagrams of the designed network from Activity 4  
 
Worksheets or activity sheets to record IP addresses, connectivity tests, and troubleshooting 
steps  
 
Sample scenario cards to guide network configuration , Step-by-step Packet Tracer configuration 
guide, Pre-configured sample network files for reference 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Demonstration: Teacher shows step-by-step device placement, cabling, and IP configuration  
 
Guided Practice: Learners replicate the network simulation on their computers with teacher 
support  
 
Hands-On Learning: Learners assign IPs, connect devices, and test connectivity using ping  
 
Problem-Solving & Troubleshooting: Identify and fix connection issues using logical steps  
 
Collaborative Learning: Pair work to discuss configuration choices and troubleshoot together 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Using Cisco Packet Tracer (free educational software), learners build a digital version of the network 
they designed in Activity 4. Guide them through placing devices from the Device Panel, connecting 
them with the correct cable type (straight-through for switch-to-computer connections, crossover for 
switch-to-switch connections – or use the "Automatically Choose Connection Type" option for 
beginners), and assigning IP addresses. For each PC: open the Desktop tab, select IP Configuration, 
and enter an IP address and subnet mask. Test connectivity by opening the Command Prompt on one 
PC and using the ping command (e.g., ping 192.168.1.2) to verify that packets reach another device. 
If ping fails, guide learners to troubleshoot by checking: (1) Is the cable connected? (2) Are the IP 
addresses in the same subnet? (3) Is any device powered off? 
💡 Content Tip: Packet Tracer’s Simulation Mode is an outstanding teaching tool for visualising 
abstract networking concepts. In Simulation Mode, learners can send a single packet and watch it 
travel through the network step by step, seeing exactly which device it passes through and how the 
switch’s MAC address table directs it to the correct port. This makes the otherwise invisible process of 
data switching tangible and observable. Key concepts to reinforce during configuration: each device 
needs a unique IP address in the same subnet to communicate. The subnet mask (typically 
255.255.255.0 for a simple /24 network) defines the boundary of the network – devices with different 
subnet addresses cannot communicate without a router. The ping command is the universal first 
diagnostic tool in networking: if ping succeeds, the basic network path is working.

Activity 6: Building Physical Topologies and Configuring a LAN 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 
 
Computer lab arranged with group workstations for hands-on LAN building  
 
Space for moving around safely with cables and networking hardware  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Computers with network interface cards  
 
Switches, routers, RJ45 cables (created in Activity 3)  
 
Projector/board for demonstration of IP settings and troubleshooting  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Safe, collaborative environment fostering practical application, observation, and problem-solving  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Ethernet cables (UTP/STP) previously created by learners  
 
Network switches (one per group of 3–4 learners) Switches and routers for LAN connections  
 
Computers for workstations  
 
Projector/board for live demonstration of IP configuration  
 
Worksheets to record IP addresses, ping test results, and troubleshooting notes  
 
Printed guides for step-by-step LAN setup  
 
Workstations or laptops / Optional: a router for the inter-LAN extension challenge 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Demonstration: Teacher shows IP configuration, LAN setup, and ping testing  
 
Hands-On Learning: Groups physically connect computers to switches and assign IPs  
 
Guided Practice: Teacher supports learners in troubleshooting connectivity issues  
 
Collaborative Learning: Groups work together to connect LANs through a router  
 
Problem-Solving: Diagnose failures such as missing default gateways and fix issues 
▪ How to Conduct: 
This is the culminating practical activity, combining cable creation (Activity 3), topology design (Activity 
4), and IP configuration (Activity 5) into a physical build. Each group uses the cables they created in 
Activity 3 to connect three workstations to a switch, establishing a physical Star LAN. With teacher 
guidance, each group assigns IP addresses manually to each computer (right-click Network icon > 
Properties > TCP/IP Settings) ensuring they are on the same subnet (e.g., 192.168.10.1, 192.168.10.2, 
192.168.10.3, all with subnet mask 255.255.255.0). Test connectivity using the ping command from 
each computer. Once the basic LAN works, extend the challenge: connect two groups’ LANs together 
through a router and configure the router’s IP addresses as gateways. Learners observe that without 
a default gateway configured, inter-LAN communication fails. 
💡 Content Tip: A physical build session is where conceptual understanding becomes visceral, real 
competence. When a learner’s ping command returns "Reply from 192.168.10.2" for the first time, the 
abstract concept of network addressing becomes concrete. A critical classroom management tip: label 
all cables with their destination (e.g., "PC1 to Port 1") before starting the build. Unlabelled cables in a 
multi-group session become impossible to troubleshoot. The most common physical errors are: (1) 
cable connected to the wrong port (switch ports versus router WAN ports look similar); (2) cable not 
fully seated (check the RJ45 clip is clicked in); (3) IP address typo (192.168.10.1 vs 192.168.10.11). 
These are exactly the errors a professional network technician encounters daily – learning to diagnose 
and fix them systematically is a highly transferable real-world skill.

TOPIC 2.5: CYBER SECURITY 
Overview 
Cyber security focuses on keeping digital devices and information safe from threats such as viruses, 
hacking, and online scams. In schools, homes, and workplaces in Zambia, people rely on computers and 
mobile phones for communication and services. Learning cyber security helps learners protect their data, 
avoid cybercrime, and use technology safely and responsibly. 
General Competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking 
Ability to examine digital systems, identify weaknesses, and interpret 
security risks effectively. 
Collaboration and 
Communication 
Working with peers to discuss vulnerabilities, share insights, and 
develop security policies. 
Critical Thinking and 
Problem-Solving 
Evaluating threats and applying logical solutions to prevent or mitigate 
cyber-attacks. 
Cybersecurity Awareness 
Understanding potential cyber threats, best practices, and the 
importance of data protection. 
Digital Literacy 
Knowledge of operating systems, software, hardware, and safe digital 
practices. 
Ethical and Societal 
Awareness 
Recognising ethical issues and societal implications of cyber security, 
including privacy and responsible digital behaviour. 
 
Hook 
"Imagine receiving a text message saying you've won a lottery you never entered, asking for your 
mobile money PIN to claim the prize. Would you know this is a scam? How can you protect yourself 
and your family?" (Ask: "Has anyone here or someone they know ever received a message like that? 
What happened?") 
Key Terms  
Cyber Security: The practice of protecting computers, networks, programs, and data from digital 
attack, damage, or unauthorised access. 
Malware: Malicious software designed to harm, exploit, or compromise a computer system (e.g., 
viruses, ransomware, spyware). 
Phishing: A cyber-attack where attackers disguise themselves as trustworthy entities to trick 
individuals into revealing sensitive information. 
Hacking: Gaining unauthorised access to a computer system or network. 
Firewall: A network security system that monitors and controls incoming/outgoing network traffic 
based on security rules. 
Antivirus: Software designed to detect, prevent, and remove malicious software from a computer. 
Confidentiality: A principle of cyber security ensuring that data is only accessible to authorised users. 
Integrity: A principle ensuring that data is accurate, complete, and has not been tampered with. 
Availability: A principle ensuring that data and systems are accessible to authorised users when 
needed. 
Two-Factor Authentication (2FA): A security process requiring two forms of identification to verify 
identity – adding an extra layer of protection beyond a password.

Sub-Topic 2.5.1: Introduction to Cyber Security 
Introduction: This sub-topic introduces cyber security as the practice of protecting computers, networks, 
and data from unauthorised access, attacks, or damage. It includes understanding cyber security and its 
importance in digital systems, identifying vulnerabilities in operating systems, software, and hardware, and 
using tools and technologies to improve security. It also involves developing policies and practices that 
help protect data, devices, and networks from cyber threats, ensuring safe and responsible use of 
technology. 
Specific Competence(s) – Learners will: 
• 
2.5.1.1 Utilise cybersecurity tools and technologies 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 1: Discussing Cyber Security and Its Importance in Digital Systems 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 
 
Classroom arranged for small group discussions and board presentation  
 
Tables or desks grouped to allow collaboration  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Projector/board to display anonymised cybercrime stories, CIA Triad diagram, and examples  
 
Optional computers for learners to research additional local cyber incidents  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Supportive, reflective space encouraging discussion, critical thinking, and ethical reasoning  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Projector/board for story presentation and CIA Triad diagram template for group activity 
 
Worksheets or activity sheets for CIA principle assignments  
 
Charts/posters to summarise definitions and prevention strategies / Charts with key terms for 
reference 
 
Markers, pens, and sticky notes for class discussion and group work  
 
Printed anonymised cybercrime scenario case studies cards (mobile money scam and social 
media hack) 
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Discussion-Based Learning: Analyse real-life cybercrime cases and reflect on impacts  
 
Collaborative Learning: Groups define CIA principles, provide examples, and suggest 
prevention  
 
Active Learning: Groups present findings and build a class CIA Triad diagram together  
 
Problem-Solving: Identify threats and propose strategies to prevent similar incidents  
 
Guided Inquiry: Teacher facilitates linking local examples to the theoretical CIA framework 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Begin with a class reading of two anonymised real-world cybercrime stories relevant to Zambia: a 
mobile money scam (e.g., a fake Airtel/MTN text claiming a prize) and a social media account hack. 
After reading, ask: "Who was harmed? How were they harmed? Could it have been prevented?" 
Introduce the CIA Triad as the framework for thinking about what was violated: the mobile money scam 
violated Confidentiality (the PIN was exposed to an unauthorised person); a data breach violates 
Integrity (data was altered); a Denial-of-Service attack violates Availability (the system was made 
inaccessible). Groups of three are each assigned one CIA principle and must (a) write a one-sentence 
definition, (b) give a local example of a threat that would violate it, and (c) suggest one prevention 
strategy. Groups present their findings and the class builds a master CIA Triad diagram on the board.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
 
💡 Content Tip: The CIA Triad – Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability – is the foundational framework 
of information security. Every cyber threat can be analysed through this lens. Confidentiality threats include 
data breaches and phishing (unauthorised access to private data). Integrity threats include malware that 
modifies files or man-in-the-middle attacks that alter data in transit. Availability threats include Distributed 
Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks that overwhelm a server and ransomware that locks users out of their 
own files. Grounding this in local, relatable examples is essential: mobile money scams (confidentiality), 
someone editing a school database to change marks (integrity), and a school’s exam system going offline 
during tests (availability) make abstract principles tangible for learners in Zambia. 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 2: Investigating Cyber Vulnerabilities in Operating Systems, Software, and 
Hardware 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 
 
Classroom arranged for paired work with space for discussion and debrief  
 
Tables or desks grouped to facilitate collaboration  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Projector/board to display Chanda’s profile and vulnerability categories  
 
Optional computers or tablets for researching safe practices  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Supportive, reflective environment encouraging critical observation, prioritisation, and ethical 
decision-making  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Printed fictional profile and checklist of vulnerability categories  
 
Worksheets or activity sheets to record identified vulnerabilities and fixes  
 
Projector/board for class debrief and discussion  
 
Charts/posters summarising digital security best practices  
 
Pens, markers, and sticky notes for group work  
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Case-Based Learning: Analyse a fictional scenario to identify real-world vulnerabilities  
 
Collaborative Learning: Pairs discuss and prioritise vulnerabilities, suggesting fixes  
 
Problem-Solving: Decide which vulnerability to address first and justify reasoning  
 
Guided Discussion: Class shares findings and teacher facilitates linking to broader 
cybersecurity concepts  
 
Active Learning: Engage in practical decision-making about securing digital accounts and 
devices 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Frame this activity as a "digital audit". Learners are given a fictional profile: "Chanda is a Form 2 
learner. She uses one password (chanda2010) for her email, Facebook, and Zamtel account. Her 
phone’s screen lock is off. She hasn’t updated her phone’s software in 8 months. She clicked a link in 
a WhatsApp message from an unknown number." Working in pairs, learners identify every vulnerability 
in Chanda’s digital habits from a checklist of categories: password strength, password reuse, physical 
security (unlocked device), software updates, and suspicious links. Pairs then prioritise the 
vulnerabilities from most to least dangerous and recommend a specific fix for each. The class debriefs 
by discussing: "Which vulnerability would you fix first if you could only do one? Why?"

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: Think of cyber security like securing a home. A weak password is an unlocked door. 
Password reuse is using the same key for your house, car, and workplace – if one is stolen, everything is 
compromised. Outdated software is a broken window – attackers know about the gap and will exploit it 
before you fix it. An unlocked phone is leaving your house door wide open. Clicking unknown links is letting 
a stranger walk in. Software updates are not cosmetic changes; they are security patches that close known 
vulnerabilities. A practical metric: the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) reports that 
over 80% of successful cyber-attacks exploit either weak passwords, password reuse, or unpatched 
software – all of which are free to fix with good habits. Facilitating a learner self-audit of their own digital 
habits ("do you use the same password for multiple accounts?") makes the lesson personally relevant and 
motivating. 
 
Learning Activities 
Activity 3: Using Cyber Security Tools and Technologies 
 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 
 
Classroom organised into three clearly defined stations for rotation  
 
Seating arranged in small groups to allow smooth movement between stations  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Computers with internet access for password testing  
 
Antivirus software installed or screenshots available  
 
Projector/board to display instructions and key concepts  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Interactive and engaging environment promoting hands-on exploration, curiosity, and reflective 
thinking  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Computers with internet access (password strength testing) for Station 1 
 
Antivirus software installed or demo screenshots for Station 2 
 
Printed 2FA role-play cards and numbered code cards  
 
Worksheets for recording observations (password strength, antivirus features, 2FA process)  
 
Charts/posters showing characteristics of strong passwords and 2FA concepts  
 
Pens, markers, and sticky notes  
 
 Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Station Rotation Method: Structured movement between password testing, antivirus 
exploration, and 2FA simulation  
 
Experiential Learning: Direct interaction with tools to understand cybersecurity concepts  
 
Collaborative Learning: Group work to analyse results and share findings  
 
Demonstration: Teacher models use of antivirus tools and explains 2FA  
 
Reflective Discussion: Learners analyse outcomes and discuss security implications 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Structure this as a three-station practical rotation (10 minutes per station, groups rotate). Station 1 – 
Passwords: Learners use an online password strength meter (e.g., howsecureismypassword.net) to 
test three passwords of increasing complexity (e.g., "cat", "Catfish12", "C@tf!sh#2026Zambia"). They 
record the time-to-crack estimate for each and identify what elements of a strong password (length, 
uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols) made the difference. Station 2 – Antivirus: Learners explore 
the interface of an installed antivirus program (or a demo screenshot). They identify: the last scan date, 
what to do if a threat is found, and how to schedule automatic scans. Station 3 – 2FA Simulation: Using 
printed scenario cards, learners role-play a 2FA login: they "enter a password" (read a card), then 
"receive an SMS code" (teacher hands them a numbered card), and must use both to "log in". Debrief: 
what happens if only one factor is compromised?

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
 
💡 Content Tip: A strong password should be at least 12 characters long and include a mix of 
uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and special symbols. The single most impactful 
improvement, however, is length: every additional character increases the time required for a brute-
force attack exponentially. A 6-character password can be cracked in seconds; a 16-character 
passphrase takes billions of years. A passphrase (e.g., "Zambezi!River2026") is both strong and 
memorable. Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) adds a second layer: even if an attacker steals the 
password, they cannot log in without the second factor (a code sent to the user’s phone). This is why 
2FA is the single most effective security measure an individual can enable. Real-world connection: 
Airtel Money, MTN MoMo, and most banks use 2FA via SMS codes – learners are likely already using 
it without knowing the term.

Activity 4: Developing Policies to Protect Data, Devices, and Networks from Cyber Threats 
 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom) Environment 
 
Arrange desks in small collaborative groups of 4–5 learners to support discussion and drafting.  
 
Provide wall space or board space where groups can display their drafted AUP rules.  
 
Create a “policy presentation corner” where groups present and defend their rules.  






 
Demonstrate screen-locking and password protection on an actual computer for practical 
understanding.



cafés).  
 
Encourage learners to reflect on real situations where cyber rules were broken and 
consequences occurred.  






 
Voting slips for consolidation and selection of best rules.  
 
Sticky notes for peer feedback.  


 
Printed template showing: Rule (Do), Explanation, Consequence format.  
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Problem-Based Learning: Present the task as a real request from the headteacher to give 
authenticity and purpose.  
 
Collaborative Learning: Learners work in groups to draft and refine rules together.  
 
Discussion Method: Facilitate class discussion to analyse and improve drafted rules.  
 
Demonstration Method: Show practical examples of locking screens and managing passwords.  
 
Peer Review: Groups evaluate each other’s rules before voting.  
 
Participatory Decision-Making: Learners vote and co-create the final consolidated AUP.  
 
Reflective Learning: Guide learners to explain why each rule exists and its consequences.

Form 2 – Secondary Education 
 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Frame this as a real brief: "The headteacher has asked your class to create the school’s first Computer Lab 
Acceptable Use Policy (AUP). This will be printed and displayed in the lab, and all learners must sign it." Learners 
work in groups to draft five to seven rules covering: password management (no sharing), software use (no 
downloading unapproved software), physical security (locking screens when leaving), reporting (what to do if they 
see a threat or inappropriate content), and privacy (not accessing other learners’ accounts). Groups must write 
each rule in both positive form ("Do: Lock your screen when you leave the computer") and explain the 
consequence of not following it ("Unlocked screens allow others to access your account and private data"). 
Groups share their drafts, the class votes on the best rules, and a final consolidated policy is created together 
and displayed in the lab. 
 
💡 Content Tip: A well-designed Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) is a foundational security document for any 
organisation – from a school computer lab to a multinational company. Effective AUPs share three characteristics: 
they are specific ("Use a password of at least 8 characters" is better than "Use a strong password"), they are 
enforceable (there must be a realistic way to check compliance), and they include consequences (learners 
understand why each rule exists). Positive framing ("Do" before "Don’t") is more effective psychologically. The 
creation process itself is pedagogically valuable: when learners write the rules, they own them and are more likely 
to follow them. Connect this to the broader concept of cyber security culture: technology alone cannot secure a 
system – human behaviour is always the final layer of defence. 
 
 
Expected Standard 
Cybersecurity tools and technologies utilised accordingly.

1. (Identifying Threats) What type of cyber threat is this message an example of? 
2. (Policy Development) What are two specific pieces of advice you would give your grandmother to 
help her avoid falling for this kind of trick in the future?

TOPIC 2.6: ALGORITHMS 
Overview 
This topic introduces learners to the fundamental principles of algorithms. Learners will explore how to 
design, represent, and refine step-by-step procedures to solve computational and real-world problems. 
Emphasis is placed on algorithmic thinking, logic development, and the use of visual tools like flowcharts 
and pseudocode. 
General Competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking 
Ability to break down problems and understand the steps required 
to form algorithms. 
Algorithmic Thinking 
Ability to design step-by-step procedures and logical sequences to 
solve problems efficiently. 
Critical Thinking and 
Problem-Solving 
Evaluating different algorithm solutions and selecting the most 
effective approach. 
Creativity and Innovation 
Developing unique and efficient algorithms and improving existing 
solutions. 
Digital Literacy 
Understanding how algorithms are applied in digital systems and 
computing environments. 
Technical Proficiency 
Applying skills in flowcharts, pseudocode, and algorithm 
implementation accurately. 
 
Hook 
"What if you could write a recipe for a computer to solve any problem – from sorting your playlist to 
finding the shortest route to school? How do you think computers 'think' step-by-step?" (Start with: 
"How would you write instructions for someone to make nshima?" Have a volunteer follow the 
instructions literally to highlight the need for precision.) 
Key Terms  
Algorithm: A precise, step-by-step, finite set of instructions designed to perform a specific task or 
solve a problem. 
Flowchart: A visual diagram that represents an algorithm using standard symbols (ovals, rectangles, 
diamonds) to show the flow of steps and decisions. 
Pseudocode: A simplified, informal way of writing an algorithm’s steps using plain language and basic 
programming structures, without strict syntax rules. 
Sequence: The logical order in which steps in an algorithm are executed, one after another. 
Selection (Decision): A control structure that chooses between different paths based on a condition 
(e.g., "IF it’s raining, THEN take an umbrella"). 
Iteration (Loop): A control structure that repeats a block of steps until a specific condition is met. 
Optimisation: The process of refining an algorithm to make it more efficient (e.g., reducing the number 
of steps or memory used). 
Input/Output: Data received by an algorithm (input) or produced by it (output). 
Process: A single step or operation within an algorithm that transforms data. 
Efficiency: A measure of how well an algorithm uses resources like time and memory.

Sub-Topic 2.6.1: Fundamentals of Algorithms 
Introduction: This sub-topic introduces algorithms as step-by-step instructions that solve problems. 
Teachers should explain the different types of algorithms and discuss their key characteristics, helping 
learners understand how algorithms work in computing. Guide learners to design flowcharts and write 
pseudocode, showing how algorithms can be applied to solve computational problems. Encourage 
learners to refine and optimise their algorithms to improve efficiency and accuracy. Introduce various 
algorithm design techniques and provide practical examples so learners can practice creating and applying 
algorithms effectively. 
Specific Competence(s) – Learners will: 
• 
2.6.1.1 Design algorithms 
Learning Activities 
 
Activity 1: Identifying Different Types of Algorithms 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 
 
Classroom arranged for interactive discussion and pair work  
 
Open space at the front for demonstration (role-play of the nshima-making algorithm)  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Projector/board to display examples of algorithms (recipes, routines, GPS routes)  
 
Optional digital devices for viewing additional examples  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Engaging and relaxed environment encouraging participation, curiosity, and humour-driven 
learning  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Algorithm classification cards (recipe, morning routine, GPS route) 
 
Nshima ingredients for the literal interpretation activity 
 
Board/flip chart for writing instructions and classifications  
 
Worksheets for classification activity / Chalkboard or whiteboard for the class debrief 
 
Charts/posters showing types of algorithms (sequential, selection, iteration)  
 
Everyday object examples (traffic light, ATM process illustrations)  
 
Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Demonstration: Role-play to show importance of precise instructions in algorithms  
 
Discussion-Based Learning: Class discussion on everyday algorithms and their importance  
 
Collaborative Learning: Pair work to classify types of algorithms  
 
Inquiry-Based Learning: “Spot the algorithm” activity to explore real-life applications  
 
Active Learning: Learners engage through participation, observation, and classification 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Open with the nshima-making algorithm from the hook. Ask a volunteer to give you instructions for 
making nshima, and follow them literally and robotically (e.g., if they say "add water", ask "how much 
water? Where is the water? Which hand do I use?"). This reliably generates laughter while powerfully 
demonstrating that an algorithm must be precise and unambiguous. Then transition to more 
computational examples. Present three algorithm cards: a recipe (sorting algorithm), morning routine 
(sequential algorithm), and GPS route (pathfinding algorithm). In pairs, learners classify each as 
primarily sequential (steps in order), selection-based (requires decisions), or iteration-based (repeats 
steps). Conclude with a "spot the algorithm" challenge: identify hidden algorithms in everyday objects 
– a traffic light timer, a vending machine payment sequence, an ATM withdrawal process.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: An algorithm is defined by four essential characteristics: Finiteness (it must stop – an 
algorithm that runs forever is not an algorithm), Definiteness (each step must be clear and 
unambiguous – "add some water" is not definite; "add 500ml of water" is), Input/Output (it takes in data 
and produces a result), and Effectiveness (every step must be doable with the available resources). 
The nshima/sandwich activity is pedagogically powerful precisely because it fails on definiteness – the 
instructions are ambiguous. Use this failure productively: "What did you need to add to make the 
instruction unambiguous?" The answer is the definition of algorithmic thinking: specify the exact action, 
the exact object, and the exact condition.

Activity 2: Discussing Characteristics of Algorithms

 
Worksheets for evaluating algorithm characteristics  


effectiveness)  
 
Writing materials (exercise books, pens, markers)  
 
Board/flip chart for class discussion





Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Discussion-Based Learning: Analyse and evaluate given algorithm examples  
 
Collaborative Learning: Groups create and assess algorithms together  
 
Peer Assessment: Groups exchange and evaluate each other’s algorithms  
 
Problem-Solving: Identify errors and propose improvements to algorithms  
 
Guided Inquiry: Teacher facilitates linking examples to key characteristics 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Present four instruction sets on the board and ask learners to evaluate each against the four algorithm 
characteristics (finiteness, definiteness, input/output, effectiveness). Example 1: a good algorithm 
(clear steps with a definite end). Example 2: an infinite loop ("Keep counting up from 1" – violates 
finiteness). Example 3: ambiguous steps ("Sort the pile somehow" – violates definiteness). Example 
4: an impossible step ("Divide by zero" – violates effectiveness). For each example, learners identify 
which characteristic(s) are violated and propose a fix. Consolidate with a group task: each group writes 
a five-step algorithm for an assigned everyday task (boiling water for tea, crossing a road safely, paying 
at a till) and then swaps with another group to evaluate their algorithm against all four characteristics. 
Groups provide written feedback identifying any violations.

Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: A common misconception to address directly: a long set of instructions is not 
necessarily an algorithm, and a short one is not necessarily simple. What makes something an 
algorithm is the combination of all four characteristics working together. For example, "Think of a 
number between 1 and 10" is a valid algorithm step (it has finite options and a clear output), while "Do 
your best" is not (it is not definite – what does ‘best’ mean in computational terms?). The concept of 
definiteness is especially important to connect forward to programming: in Python, for example, the 
computer executes exactly what is written, not what you meant to write. Imprecise thinking at the 
algorithm stage leads directly to buggy code at the programming stage.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Introduce flowchart symbols with a "symbol gallery" – post large cards on the wall, each showing one 
symbol, its name, and its function. Then demonstrate designing a flowchart live for a familiar process: 
"Checking if a number is even or odd." Think aloud while drawing: "I start with an oval labelled START. 
Then a rectangle: INPUT a number. Then a diamond: Is number ÷ 2 remainder = 0? If YES, a rectangle: 
OUTPUT ‘Even’. If NO, a rectangle: OUTPUT ‘Odd’. Then an oval: END." Learners copy this, then 
independently design a flowchart for: checking if a learner has passed an exam (score ≥ 50), and 
deciding whether to go to school (Is it raining? Do I have an umbrella?). The parallel pseudocode task: 
after drawing the flowchart, learners translate it into structured plain-English pseudocode, using 
IF/THEN/ELSE for decisions and FOR/WHILE for loops.

💡 Content Tip: Standard flowchart symbols carry precise meanings that should be taught and 
enforced from the start: Oval (Terminator): used only for START and END. Rectangle 
(Process): any action or calculation step (e.g., Set Total = Price × Quantity). Diamond 
(Decision): a question with exactly two outcomes, YES/NO or TRUE/FALSE. Parallelogram 
(Input/Output): receiving data from a user (INPUT) or displaying a result (OUTPUT). Arrows: 
show the direction of flow and must always have arrowheads. A diagram without these 
conventions is not a flowchart – it is a sketch. Enforcing correct symbol usage from the 
beginning builds professional precision and prepares learners for qualifications where incorrect 
symbols lose marks. Provide a laminated symbol reference card for learners to keep in their 
notebooks.

Activity 4: Applying Algorithms to Computational Problems 
Learning Environment Setup



Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Demonstration: Teacher models solving problems using flowcharts, pseudocode, and trace 
tables  
 
Guided Practice: Learners work through Level 1 and Level 2 tasks with support  
 
Independent Practice: Learners solve Level 3 problems using loops and trace tables  
 
Problem-Solving Approach: Apply algorithms to real computational problems  
 
Analytical Learning: Use trace tables to test and verify algorithm correctness 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Progress through three problem levels. Level 1 – Sequential: Design an algorithm to calculate a 
learner’s average score from five subjects. Learners write both a flowchart and pseudocode, using the 
INPUT, PROCESS (SUM ÷ 5), and OUTPUT structure. Level 2 – Selection: Design an algorithm to 
award a grade (A, B, C, D, F) based on a score. This requires nested IF/ELSE IF conditions. Learners 
trace the algorithm with three different test inputs (95, 65, 35) to verify it produces the correct grade 
for each. Level 3 – Iteration: Design an algorithm to find the highest score in a list of 30 learners’ marks. 
This requires a loop with a running maximum variable. Introduce the "trace table" technique: a table 
with columns for each variable (score, max, counter) that learners fill in manually for the first five 
iterations to verify the algorithm’s logic before trusting the result.

Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: The three fundamental control structures of all algorithms (and all 
programming languages) are Sequence (steps executed in order), Selection (IF condition 
THEN action ELSE alternative action), and Iteration (WHILE condition DO steps / FOR i FROM 
1 TO n DO steps). Any computational problem can be solved using combinations of these 
three structures. The trace table technique is an essential but underused skill: manually 
stepping through an algorithm with specific inputs to verify it produces the correct outputs 
before implementing it in code. It is the algorithm-level equivalent of unit testing in professional 
software development. When an algorithm fails a trace table, the error is found and fixed at the 
logical level – which is far faster than debugging compiled code.

Activity 5: Refining and Optimising Algorithms 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 


Technology Environment:

Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Comparison sheets (Linear Search vs Binary Search)  
 
Sample algorithms with intentional logical errors for the debugging task 


 
Worksheets for recording observations and conclusions  
 
Charts/posters showing concepts of efficiency (time and steps)  
 
Writing materials (exercise books, pens, rulers)  
 
Side-by-side algorithm comparison sheets (Linear Search vs. Binary Search) 
 
 Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Comparative Analysis: Learners compare two algorithms solving the same problem  
 
Guided Discovery: Teacher leads learners to observe differences in efficiency  
 
Collaborative Learning: Pairs/groups trace algorithms and discuss findings  
 
Problem-Solving: Identify, analyse, and correct logical errors in algorithms  
 
Analytical Learning: Use trace tables to evaluate performance and correctness 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Present two algorithms that solve the same problem: searching a list of 100 names for a specific name. 
Algorithm A (Linear Search): starts at the first name and checks every name one by one until the target 
is found – in the worst case, 100 comparisons. Algorithm B (Binary Search): requires the list to be 
sorted first, then repeatedly halves the search space – in the worst case, only 7 comparisons for a 
100-item list (log₂ 100 ≈ 7). Learners trace both algorithms with a small 10-name sorted list to observe 
the difference. Discuss: "Is Binary Search always better? What is its limitation?" (It requires a pre-
sorted list.) Present a third task: an algorithm with a logical error (e.g., a loop that counts to 11 instead 
of 10, or an IF condition that uses “>” instead of “≥”, causing off-by-one errors). Learners identify and 
fix the error and explain what the consequence of the bug would have been in a real system.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: Algorithm efficiency is measured in terms of time complexity (how many steps does it 
take as the input size grows?) and space complexity (how much memory does it use?). At this level, 
learners need not study Big-O notation formally, but developing the intuition for efficiency is important. 
The Linear Search vs. Binary Search comparison is an ideal intuition-builder: doubling the list size 
doubles the steps for linear search, but only adds one step for binary search. This exponential 
difference becomes enormous at scale – searching a list of one million names takes up to 1,000,000 
steps linearly but at most 20 steps with binary search. The practical lesson: choosing the right algorithm 
for the problem is often more impactful than having the fastest computer.

Activity 6: Using Different Algorithm Design Techniques 
Learning Environment Setup 
 
Artificial (Physical – Classroom): 
 
Classroom arranged for group work and presentations  
 
Technology Environment: 
 
Projector/board to demonstrate examples of algorithm design techniques  
 
Optional computers for designing and comparing solutions  
 
Natural Environment: 
 
Interactive environment encouraging discussion, creativity, and real-life problem-solving  
 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
 
Problem context cards for Brute Force, Divide and Conquer, and Greedy technique activities 
 
Flowchart templates and pseudocode reference guides 
 
Example comparison sheets for different techniques, Charts/posters explaining algorithm design 
techniques, Writing materials (exercise books, pens, markers)  
 
 Suggested Teaching Methods 
 
Collaborative Learning: Groups solve problems using assigned algorithm design techniques  
 
Problem-Based Learning: Real-life scenarios used to apply different techniques  
 
Demonstration: Teacher explains and models each design technique  
 
Comparative Discussion: Class evaluates effectiveness of different approaches  
 
Presentation Method: Groups present solutions and justify chosen techniques 
▪ How to Conduct: 
Introduce three design techniques through real Zambian problem contexts. Brute Force: "Find the 
cheapest combination of items at a market that totals exactly K50." Explain that brute force checks 
every possible combination – it is simple to implement but slow for large inputs. Divide and Conquer: 
"You have an alphabetically sorted list of 256 names. Find ‘Mwamba’." Demonstrate binary search as 
a classic divide-and-conquer algorithm – split the list in half, decide which half contains the target, and 
repeat. Greedy Algorithm: "A minibus is taking on passengers at a bus stop. It seats 14. Passengers 
board largest-group-first to fill the seats most efficiently." A greedy algorithm always makes the locally 
optimal choice at each step. Groups solve one problem using their assigned technique, present their 
flowchart/pseudocode to the class, and the class discusses: "Would a different technique solve this 
problem? Would it be faster or slower?" 
💡 Content Tip: Design technique selection is as important as the algorithm itself. Brute Force is simple 
and always finds the correct answer, but is only practical for small input sizes. Divide and Conquer 
(binary search, merge sort) dramatically reduces the number of comparisons needed by repeatedly 
halving the problem space – it is efficient for large, sorted datasets. Greedy algorithms make the locally 
optimal choice at each step and are fast and simple, but do not always find the globally optimal solution 
(a classic example: the coin-change problem with unusual denominations). Connecting these 
techniques to real contexts in Zambia – bus scheduling, market pricing, patient queue management at 
a clinic – grounds abstract computer science in the learners’ lived experience and demonstrates the 
transferable value of computational thinking.

Example of Summative Assessment: 
Scenario (Project: “The Efficient Daily Task”): A security guard at a secondary school in Chipata 
needs to check that all 12 classroom doors are locked at the end of the day. He currently walks to 
each door in a random order, often passing the same doors multiple times and wasting time. 
Task: 
1. Flowchart: Design a flowchart for a more efficient algorithm. Assume classrooms are in two 
straight lines. Your algorithm should start at the first classroom, check if it’s locked, and then 
move to the next one until all 12 are checked. 
2. Pseudocode: Write the pseudocode for the same process. Use a loop (e.g., FOR each room 
FROM 1 TO 12) to represent the repetition. 
3. Refinement Report: In a short paragraph, explain why your algorithm is more efficient than the 
guard’s random method. Think about time saved and how it ensures he never misses a 
classroom.

Summary – Key Points Recap: 
• 
An algorithm is a precise, step-by-step procedure to solve a problem. 
• 
A valid algorithm must be well-defined, finite, and produce consistent results. 
• 
Algorithms are built from three fundamental control structures: Sequence, Selection (if-else), 
and Iteration (loops). 
• 
Flowcharts are visual diagrams and pseudocode is structured plain-English text used to 
represent algorithms. 
• 
Algorithmic thinking is a critical skill for breaking down complex problems. 
• 
Refining/Optimising an algorithm improves its correctness, clarity, and efficiency.

TOPIC 2.7: WEB DEVELOPMENT 
Overview 
Web development involves the creation and styling of digital content to ensure it is functional, accessible, 
and visually engaging. This topic focuses on Cascading Style Sheets (CSS), the essential design tool that 
allows developers to control the presentation of HTML structures. 
General Competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking 
Analysing design requirements and selecting appropriate CSS 
methods and properties. 
Collaboration and 
Communication 
Working with others to design, review, and improve web page 
layouts. 
Critical Thinking and Problem-
Solving 
Identifying and correcting layout and styling issues in web pages. 
Creativity and Innovation 
Designing attractive and user-friendly web pages using CSS 
styling techniques. 
Digital Literacy 
Understanding how CSS integrates with HTML to create styled 
web content. 
Ethical and Societal Awareness Ensuring accessibility and inclusive web design for all users. 
Technical Proficiency 
Applying CSS rules, selectors, and properties accurately. 
Web Development Skills 
Developing web pages using CSS concepts such as selectors, 
box model, and layouts. 
 
Hook 
"Why do two different websites look completely different even if they are both about the same topic? 
Who decides the colours and fonts?" (Show a plain HTML page with no styling, then apply a CSS file 
and transform its appearance completely. The contrast is very powerful.) 
 
Key Terms  
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets): A language used to describe the visual presentation of a web page, 
controlling layout, colours, fonts, and more. 
CSS Selector: A pattern used to select and target specific HTML elements to style (e.g., h1, 
.className, #idName). 
Property: A specific style attribute to change, such as color, font-size, or margin. 
Value: The setting assigned to a CSS property (e.g., setting color to blue). 
Inline CSS: CSS applied directly to a single HTML element using the style attribute. 
Internal CSS: CSS placed within a <style> tag in the page’s header. 
External CSS: CSS in a separate .css file linked to an HTML page – best practice for multi-page sites. 
Box Model: A CSS concept describing every element as a rectangular box with content, padding 
(space around content), a border, and a margin (space outside the border). 
Responsive Design: A web design approach that makes pages render well on a variety of devices 
and screen sizes.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2 | Ministry of Education, Zambia | 2026 Form 2 – Secondary Education Sub-Topic 2.7.1: Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) Concepts Introduction: This sub-topic introduces Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) and how they are used to style web pages. Teachers should guide learners to identify the different types of CSS: inline, internal, and external, and explain the basic structure of a CSS rule. 💡 Content Tip: The relationship between HTML and CSS is one of the most important architectural 
concepts in web development: HTML defines the structure and semantic meaning of content (this is a 
heading, this is a paragraph, this is an image), while CSS defines the visual presentation (this heading 
is purple, this paragraph has 16px text, this image has a 2px border). This separation of concerns is a 
design principle that makes websites easier to maintain, update, and adapt. For a concrete 
demonstration of its power: the CSS Zen Garden website (csszengarden.com) shows dozens of 
radically different web designs all using exactly the same HTML file but different CSS stylesheets – 
making the point that structure and style are genuinely independent.

Activity 2: Describing the Basic Structure of a CSS Rule

▪ How to Conduct: 
Provide learners with a pre-written HTML page and a CSS file containing at least six rules. Learners 
open both files in the text editor and the browser simultaneously. Using the browser’s built-in Developer 
Tools (right-click any element > Inspect, or F12), learners click on different elements on the page and 
observe which CSS rules are highlighted in the Styles panel. The task: for each of the six CSS rules, 
learners must complete a table with four columns: (1) Selector – what element is being targeted?; (2) 
Property – what aspect is being changed?; (3) Value – what is it being changed to?; (4) Effect – what 
does this rule look like on the page? Consolidate by asking learners to write three new CSS rules from 
scratch and explain each component before applying them. The Developer Tools inspection approach 
is essential: it shows the live CSS that is actually affecting the page, not just the CSS that is written.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2 | Ministry of Education, Zambia | 2026 Form 2 – Secondary Education 💡 Content Tip: The anatomy of a CSS rule follows a strict pattern: selector { property: value; }. The selector identifies who to style (e.g., all

tags, or elements with class="highlight"). The property identifies what aspect of the element to style (e.g., color, font-size, background-color). The value defines how to style it (e.g., green, 18px, #FF0000). Multiple declarations can be stacked inside one rule: h1 { color: purple; font-size: 32px; font-weight: bold; }. A common beginner error is confusing the colour of text (the color property) with the background colour (background-color). A useful analogy: the selector is "which students in the class?", the property is "which feature of their uniform?", and the value is "what colour is it?" 💡 Content Tip: The cascade in ‘Cascading Style Sheets’ refers to the order in which conflicting CSS 
rules are resolved. When two rules target the same element, the browser applies a specificity hierarchy 
to decide which wins: Inline styles (highest specificity, override everything) beat Internal styles, which 
beat External styles. Within the same level, the rule written last in the file wins. This is why adding a 
style="" attribute to a single element will always override any class or element selector in a stylesheet. 
For professional web development, the recommended approach is always to use external stylesheets 
and avoid inline styles entirely. Inline styles make maintenance difficult because the styling is scattered 
through the HTML rather than centralised in one .css file. Teach this priority order early to prevent 
confusion when learners later find that their stylesheet rules are being ‘overridden’.

💡 Content Tip: Typography is one of the most powerful tools in web design, but beginners often 
underestimate it. Font pairing (using two complementary fonts – typically a decorative or serif font for 
headings and a clean sans-serif font for body text) significantly improves readability and visual 
hierarchy. Google Fonts (fonts.google.com) provides over 1,000 free, web-optimised fonts that can be 
added with a single <link> tag – a huge upgrade from the limited set of web-safe fonts. When using 
the color property, named colours (red, blue, green) are limited to about 140 options. Hexadecimal 
codes (#RRGGBB) provide access to over 16 million colours and are the professional standard. The 
browser Developer Tools are invaluable for experimenting: click on any element, find the color property 
in the Styles panel, and click the colour swatch to open a live colour picker that updates the page 
instantly.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Begin with a visual demonstration using a physical analogy: draw a picture frame on the board. The 
picture itself is the content. The mat inside the frame between the picture and the frame border is the 
padding. The frame itself is the border. The space between the frame and the wall (or other frames) is 
the margin. Map each physical component to its CSS equivalent. Then demonstrate the Browser 
Developer Tools box model panel: right-click any element > Inspect > click the element in the Elements 
panel > scroll to the bottom of the Styles panel to see the live box model diagram (a nested rectangle 
showing exact pixel values for margin, border, padding, and content). Learners then modify these 
values using CSS rules and observe the live effects. Set a practical challenge: layout three images in 
a row with equal spacing between them using only margin and padding – not tables.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2  |  Ministry of Education, Zambia  |  2026 
Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: The box model is the foundational layout concept of CSS and the source of many 
beginner layout frustrations. The critical distinction: padding is space inside the border (between the 
content and its border) – it takes the element’s background colour. Margin is space outside the border 
(between the element and its neighbours) – it is always transparent. Common pitfalls: (1) Adding too 
much margin causes mysterious gaps between elements. (2) Adding too much padding makes a 
container appear larger than intended. (3) Borders add to the total size of an element by default (this 
can be changed with box-sizing: border-box, a property worth introducing). The box-sizing: border-box 
rule is used by nearly every professional CSS framework (Bootstrap, Tailwind) because it makes size 
calculations far more predictable – include it in your starter stylesheet.

💡 Content Tip: Background properties are among the most versatile in CSS. The most important 
values to master: background-color provides a solid colour fill and acts as a fallback if an image fails 
to load. background-image: url(’...’) applies an image, which repeats by default (background-repeat: 
repeat). For a large hero/banner image that should fill the entire section, the combination background-
size: cover; background-position: center; background-repeat: no-repeat; is the standard professional 
pattern. The shorthand background property combines all sub-properties in one line: background: 
#3A3A3A url(’hero.jpg’) no-repeat center center / cover; The order matters and can be confusing – 
provide learners with this exact pattern as a template and explain each component, rather than 
expecting them to memorise the order from scratch.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Open with an empathy exercise: using the Accessibility settings in Chrome DevTools (or a browser extension like 
"Spectrum"), simulate how the page appears to users with different types of colour blindness (deuteranopia, 
protanopia). Learners observe whether their designed page is still readable and navigable. Introduce the WCAG 
(Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) contrast ratio standard: text must have a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 
against its background. Learners use WebAIM’s Contrast Checker (webaim.org/resources/contrastchecker) to 
test their colour combinations and adjust those that fail. Extend to responsive design: learners resize their browser 
window from full-screen to a narrow mobile width and observe where the layout "breaks" (text overflows, images 
are cropped, elements overlap). Discuss why this matters for learners in Zambia, where most internet access is 
via mobile phone.

Computer Science Teaching Module – Form 2 | Ministry of Education, Zambia | 2026 Form 2 – Secondary Education 💡 Content Tip: Web accessibility is an ethical imperative, not an optional feature. In Zambia, an estimated 1.4 million people live with some form of disability (WHO data), and globally, approximately 8% of males have some form of colour vision deficiency. A website that is only usable by people with perfect vision and the latest laptop immediately excludes a significant portion of the population. The WCAG 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text is the minimum legal standard in many countries and the professional baseline for all web projects. Additionally, in Zambia as elsewhere in Africa, the majority of internet users access the web via smartphone on a mobile data connection – designing only for a desktop screen in a computer lab creates pages that are unusable in the most common real-world context. Responsive design (using percentage widths, max-width, and CSS media queries) is therefore a practical and inclusive necessity. Expected Standard CSS concepts are applied to websites correctly. TOPIC 2.8: DATABASES 
Overview 
Databases are organised collections of data that allow for efficient storage, retrieval, and management of 
information. This topic introduces Structured Query Language (SQL), the universal standard for interacting 
with relational databases. Learners will move from understanding basic data organisation to designing 
schemas and writing SQL scripts to create and modify database structures. 
General Competence(s): 
Analytical Thinking 
Learners analyze database requirements, identify relationships, and 
structure data logically. 
Application Software Skills 
Using database management systems (DBMS) like MySQL, 
PostgreSQL, or MS Access to implement designs. 
Collaboration and 
Communication 
Working in pairs or groups to design schemas and discuss queries, 
sharing insights effectively. 
Critical Thinking and 
Problem-Solving 
Debugging SQL scripts, resolving data inconsistencies, and 
optimizing queries. 
Data Structure Skills 
Understanding tables, fields, keys, and relationships to organize and 
normalize data efficiently. 
Digital Literacy 
Navigating DBMS interfaces, writing and testing SQL queries, and 
interpreting results. 
Programming Skills 
Writing SQL scripts to create, modify, and manipulate databases 
programmatically. 
Technical Proficiency 
Applying technical knowledge to implement and manage database 
structures and queries successfully. 
. 
Hook 
"How does a supermarket in Lusaka keep track of thousands of items and know exactly when to order 
more sugar?" Follow up: "If they used one giant spreadsheet for everything, what problems might they 
run into?" – leading to the idea of organised tables. 
 
Key Terms / Words / Vocabulary 
SQL (Structured Query Language): A standard programming language used to manage and 
manipulate data in relational databases. 
Relational Database: A type of database that organises data into one or more tables that can be 
linked to each other based on common data. 
Table: A collection of related data organised into rows and columns within a database. 
Field / Column: A single piece of information within a table, representing a specific attribute (e.g., 
“FirstName”). 
Record / Row: A complete set of information about a single item or entry in a table. 
Primary Key: A unique identifier for each record in a database table. Cannot be empty and must be 
unique for every row. 
Schema: The structure or blueprint of a database, defining its tables, fields, relationships, and rules. 
Normalisation: The process of organising data in a database to reduce redundancy and improve data 
integrity.

Sub-Topic 2.8.1: Database Design and Creation 
Introduction: This sub-topic introduces database design and creation, focusing on how to organise and 
manage data effectively. Teachers should guide learners to design database schemas that define tables, 
fields, and relationships. Learners should practise writing SQL scripts to create and modify database 
structures, and learn how to query and manipulate data using SQL commands. Emphasise hands-on 
activities to help learners understand how databases store, retrieve, and manage information efficiently.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Use a physical simulation to introduce the concept before touching a computer. Create a physical 
"database" using index cards: a stack of "customer" cards (each with a name, phone number, and 
town) and a stack of "order" cards (each with an order number, item, and a customer name written on 
it). Ask learners to find all orders for a customer named "Chanda" – they physically search through the 
cards. Now ask: "If there were 10,000 cards, how long would that take?" Introduce SQL as the 
language that allows us to ask a database this exact question instantly: SELECT * FROM Orders 
WHERE CustomerName = ‘Chanda’. Move to a computer with an online SQL sandbox 
(SQLiteOnline.com or W3Schools SQL Tryit). Using a pre-populated Customers table, guide learners 
to run: SELECT * (all columns), SELECT specific columns, and SELECT WHERE to filter. Build 
progressively to INSERT (add a new customer) and UPDATE (change a customer’s phone number).

Form 2 – Secondary Education 
💡 Content Tip: SQL is the universal language for relational databases – it works on MySQL, 
PostgreSQL, Microsoft SQL Server, SQLite, and Oracle with minor differences in syntax. Frame SQL 
as a conversation with the database: SELECT is asking a question (“Show me...”), INSERT is adding 
information (“Record this...”), UPDATE is correcting information (“Change this...”), and DELETE is 
removing information (“Remove this...”). The asterisk (*) in SELECT * means “all columns” – a 
convenient shortcut but a bad practice in production systems where tables have many columns, as it 
retrieves more data than needed. Teach learners to be specific: SELECT FirstName, LastName FROM 
Customers is always better practice than SELECT * FROM Customers in professional contexts. 
Connect to the spreadsheet analogy: a database table is like a spreadsheet sheet, a row is a record, 
and a column is a field – but a database can link multiple tables, something a spreadsheet cannot do 
natively.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Return to the physical simulation: learners observed that the customer’s name was written on both the 
customer card and the order card. Ask: "What problem does this cause?" (If Chanda gets married and 
changes her name, we have to update it on every single order card – risking errors and inconsistency.) 
Introduce this as the core problem that relational database design solves: data normalisation. Explain 
the solution: give each customer a unique ID number (CustomerID). The order card now records only 
the CustomerID, not the full name. To get the customer’s name, the database “joins” the two tables 
using the matching ID. Learners then design an Entity-Relationship Diagram (ERD) for a school library 
system: a Books table (BookID, Title, Author, Year) and a BorrowingRecords table (RecordID, BookID, 
StudentID, DateBorrowed, DueDate). Use index cards physically to show the relationship before 
drawing the formal ERD. 
 
💡 Content Tip: Normalisation is the discipline of organising data to eliminate redundancy and ensure 
data integrity. The core principle at this level is First Normal Form (1NF): each cell in a table should 
contain one piece of information, and no data should be unnecessarily repeated across tables. The 
Primary Key is the cornerstone of a well-designed table: it uniquely identifies each record and cannot 
be NULL or duplicated. A Foreign Key in one table is a Primary Key from another table – it is the link 
that creates the relationship between tables. The ERD (Entity-Relationship Diagram) is the standard 
tool for planning a database before writing any SQL. It shows each table as a rectangle with its field 
names listed, and lines between tables showing the relationships (one-to-many, many-to-many). 
Sketching an ERD on paper before writing code is the database equivalent of designing a flowchart 
before programming – an investment that saves significant debugging time.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Guide learners to translate the ERD they designed in Activity 2 into executable SQL. Begin with the 
CREATE TABLE script for the Books table, explaining each component live: CREATE TABLE Books 
( – opens the table definition; BookID INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT, – creates a unique 
integer ID that increases automatically; Title VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, – creates a text field, 
maximum 100 characters, cannot be empty; Author VARCHAR(100), – creates a text field, which can 
be empty (nullable); Year INT – creates an integer field for the publication year; ); – closes the 
definition. Learners run the script in their SQL environment and verify the table was created by running 
SELECT * FROM Books (which returns an empty table – this is correct!). Then add the 
BorrowingRecords table, including a FOREIGN KEY constraint linking BookID to the Books table. 
Close with a practical extension: the librarian wants to add an ISBN number field. Learners write 
ALTER TABLE Books ADD COLUMN ISBN VARCHAR(20); 
 
💡 Content Tip: Data types in SQL are critical for ensuring data integrity and efficient storage. The 
most common types at this level: INT (whole numbers – for IDs, quantities, years), VARCHAR(n) 
(variable-length text up to n characters – for names, descriptions, codes), DATE (a calendar date in 
YYYY-MM-DD format – for due dates, birth dates), and DECIMAL(p,s) (precise decimal numbers with 
p total digits and s decimal places – for prices and amounts). The NOT NULL constraint prevents a 
field from being left empty – essential for fields that must always have a value (like a primary key or a 
person’s name). AUTO_INCREMENT (MySQL) or AUTOINCREMENT (SQLite) automatically assigns 
the next available integer as the primary key when a new record is inserted, eliminating the need to 
manually specify ID numbers. Using consistent naming conventions (CamelCase for table names: 
Books, BorrowingRecords; lowercase_underscores for field names: book_id, due_date) makes SQL 
scripts far more readable and professional.

▪ How to Conduct: 
Pre-populate the school library database with 20 book records and 15 borrowing records. Learners 
practice a progression of queries from simple to complex. Simple retrieval: SELECT * FROM Books; 
and SELECT Title, Author FROM Books;. Filtering: SELECT * FROM Books WHERE Year > 2010; 
and SELECT * FROM BorrowingRecords WHERE DueDate < ‘2026-01-01’; (overdue books). Sorting: 
SELECT * FROM Books ORDER BY Author ASC; and SELECT * FROM Books ORDER BY Year 
DESC;. Inserting: INSERT INTO Books (Title, Author, Year) VALUES (‘The River Between’, ‘Ngũgĩ wa 
Thiong’o’, 1965);. Updating: UPDATE Books SET Year = 1966 WHERE BookID = 3;. Learners 
document each query and its result in a query log table. Conclude with an open-ended analytical task: 
"Write three SQL queries that would give the librarian useful information about which books are most 
borrowed and which have overdue returns."

💡 Content Tip: The WHERE clause is the most powerful tool for data retrieval because it filters the 
result set to show only the records that are relevant. Common operators in WHERE clauses: = 
(equals), != or <> (not equals), > and < (greater than, less than), >= and <= (greater than or equal to, 
less than or equal to), LIKE ‘%pattern%’ (finds records containing a text pattern – e.g., WHERE Title 
LIKE ‘%Zambia%’ finds all books with ‘Zambia’ anywhere in the title), and BETWEEN x AND y (finds 
values in a range). The ORDER BY clause organises the output: ASC sorts ascending (A to Z, lowest 
to highest), DESC sorts descending (Z to A, highest to lowest). A practical tip: always use WHERE 
carefully with UPDATE and DELETE statements – omitting WHERE updates or deletes every row in 
the table, which is almost never the intended action. The habit of writing SELECT * FROM TableName 
WHERE condition first to preview which rows will be affected, before changing SELECT * to UPDATE 
or DELETE, is a professional safeguard.

• 
A database is an organised collection of data, managed by a DBMS.

computers. Label all devices (computers, switch, router) and assign IP 
addresses to each computer using the range 192.168.1.x.
